target.title: [Feature]: Channel Broker Phase 2A conformance harness
target.body:
## Maintainer TL;DR This stack consolidates the recurring Telegram/Discord/Slack/WhatsApp/Signal/iMessage/etc. maintenance churn behind one Channel Broker contract. OpenClaw keeps the shared semantics that repeatedly regress across native channel plugins: sessions, allowlists, routing, /verbose, streaming policy, durable final sends, receipts, retries, and auditability. Broker providers own platform mechanics: bot APIs, bridge/device state, native ids, delivery quirks, and provider capabilities. This PR is the regression shield. It turns the recurring channel pain into shared conformance: signed inbound webhooks, normalization, dedupe, durable final sends, receipts, retryable failures, preview/progress gates, and /verbose channelData. The direction is intentionally incremental: first land the versioned broker SDK/plugin foundation, then prove conformance, then prove opt-in routing for the major channels, then model long-tail and constrained providers. Only after migration paths exist should legacy native-channel feature work freeze in #86115. ## Why This Is Better Today, one fix to streaming, /verbose, or delivery behavior can make Telegram fast this week and then break Discord, Slack, or another native plugin the next week. The broker path makes those shared semantics one OpenClaw-owned contract instead of many platform-specific re-implementations. When a provider adds support for previews, threads, receipts, media, or constrained account modes, that capability can become available through the same contract to routed channels whose provider declares and passes conformance for it. ## Stack Shape ```mermaid flowchart TD U["Umbrella issue #86105: reduce maintained-channel churn"] --> P1["#86096 Phase 1: SDK + bundled broker foundation"] P1 --> P2A["#86153 Phase 2A: conformance + signed inbound"] P2A --> P2B["#86154 Phase 2B: Telegram opt-in proof"] P2B --> P2C["#86156 Phase 2C: Discord opt-in proof"] P2C --> P2D["#86157 Phase 2D: Slack opt-in proof"] P2D --> P3["#86164 Phase 3: official/app capability matrix"] P3 --> P4["#86165 Phase 4: constrained provider capabilities"] P4 -. "future, not in this stack" .-> P5["Future freeze issue #86115: legacy native-plugin freeze"] ``` | PR | Role in the consolidation | | --- | --- | | #86096 | Creates the versioned broker SDK/plugin foundation, provider routing guard, media final-send mapping, SecretRef/cancellation handling, and prefix-ownership contract. | | #86153 | Proves shared lifecycle conformance and adds signed inbound webhook registration/runtime handoff: inbound dedupe, durable final sends, receipts, retryable failures, preview/progress gates, and /verbose channelData. | | #86154 | Proves Telegram topics can route through broker targets without native Telegram internals. | | #86156 | Proves Discord DMs/channels/threads preserve conversation semantics through broker routing. | | #86157 | Proves Slack DMs/channel threads preserve direct/thread semantics through broker routing. | | #86164 | Adds official/app platform aliases, capability matrix rows, and declared delivery capability enforcement for long-tail channels. | | #86165 | Adds constrained-provider metadata for WhatsApp, Signal, iMessage, and bridge-backed providers. | ## Ownership Model ```mermaid flowchart LR Core["OpenClaw core
sessions, routing, allowlists
/verbose, streaming, retries
receipts, audit"] --> Broker["channel-broker plugin
openclaw/plugin-sdk/channel-broker
V1 protocol"] Broker --> Provider["Provider-owned broker
HTTP/webhook transport
platform adapters"] Provider --> Platforms["Telegram, Discord, Slack
Teams, Matrix, WhatsApp
Signal, iMessage, long tail"] Provider --> Broker Broker --> Core ``` ## Review Shape These PRs are intentionally linear. GitHub still shows `main` as the base for each PR so maintainers can land them one by one from fork branches; the branches themselves are stacked, and maintainers can review the focused adjacent diffs here: | PR | Focused adjacent diff | | --- | --- | | #86153 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-protocol...feat/channel-broker-conformance | | #86154 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-conformance...feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing | | #86156 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing...feat/channel-broker-discord-routing | | #86157 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-discord-routing...feat/channel-broker-slack-routing | | #86164 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-slack-routing...feat/channel-broker-official-platform-capabilities | | #86165 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-official-platform-capabilities...feat/channel-broker-constrained-platform-capabilities | ## This PR - Phase: Phase 2A - Conformance and signed inbound baseline - Role: Proves shared lifecycle conformance and adds signed inbound webhook registration/runtime handoff. - Head: `d218c81d5c` ## Current Review State This PR is live for maintainer review. Current head: `d218c81d5c`. Focused local validation for this phase is documented below. Broad validation remains owned by GitHub Actions. Native channel defaults remain unchanged unless this phase explicitly says otherwise. ## Summary - Adds the Phase 2A Channel Broker conformance baseline on top of #86096. - Extends `openclaw/plugin-sdk/channel-broker` with inbound normalization, dedupe-key, provider capability normalization, and platform capability checks. - Adds bundled broker conformance coverage for durable inbound receive, durable final send receipts, retryable receipt failures, preview/progress capability gates, and `/verbose`/tool-message `channelData`. ## Rollout Context Closes #86109 Related #86108 Related #86105 Stacked on #86096 Supersedes closed PR #86133, which was closed by the repository active-PR cap rather than by an implementation failure. This PR intentionally does not migrate Telegram, Discord, or Slack routing. Native defaults stay unchanged until the Phase 2B/2C/2D proofs land behind opt-in broker routing. For review, the focused stack diff is: https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-protocol...feat/channel-broker-conformance ## Real Behavior Proof - **Behavior or issue addressed:** The broker conformance baseline normalizes inbound events, builds durable dedupe keys, evaluates preview/progress capabilities, and sends outbound requests through the bundled broker lifecycle without platform-specific core branches. - **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout at `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`, using the Channel Broker plugin and SDK from the stacked rollout branch on May 24, 2026. - **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran a `node --import tsx --input-type=module` terminal proof that imported `src/plugin-sdk/channel-broker.ts`, created a Telegram inbound event, built the durable dedupe key, evaluated Discord preview/progress capabilities, and sent broker outbound requests through `channelBrokerPlugin.message.send.text`. - **Evidence after fix:** Terminal output excerpt from that local command: ```json { "inboundDedupeKey": "acme:bot-main:telegram:live-proof-telegram-1", "previewProgressSupported": true } ``` - **Observed result after fix:** The live SDK/plugin path produced the expected durable inbound key and capability decision outside the Vitest harness, proving the Phase 2A contract surface can carry the normalized lifecycle facts that later channel migrations consume. - **What was not tested:** Live provider network traffic and the full local suite; those remain covered by later provider proofs and GitHub Actions. ### Behavior The broker contract now has a conformance harness for the lifecycle paths that regress most often across maintained chat channels: inbound normalization/dedupe, durable final sends, provider receipts, retry/failure propagation, preview/progress capability checks, and `/verbose` tool-message metadata. ### Environment - Local checkout: `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker` - Branch: `feat/channel-broker-conformance` - Head: `d218c81d5c` (current Phase 2A head) ### Steps 1. Normalized a synthetic Telegram inbound event through the SDK and accepted it through the durable inbound receive journal. 2. Replayed the same dedupe key while pending and after completion to prove duplicate/redelivery handling. 3. Sent a synthetic Slack final message through the bundled broker runtime and verified provider receipt commit material. 4. Returned a retryable Discord provider receipt and verified the send fails with structured receipt context. 5. Built preview/finalize broker requests and asserted Discord preview/progress support through declared capabilities. 6. Passed `/verbose` tool-message policy through broker `channelData` without platform-specific branching. ### Evidence - `OPENCLAW_VITEST_INCLUDE_FILE=/Volumes/LEXAR/Codex/channel-broker-vitest-include.json pnpm exec vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts` - 1 file passed, 7 tests passed. - `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/conformance.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/secret-contract.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-channel-broker.config.ts` - 3 files passed, 10 tests passed. - `pnpm exec tsc --noEmit -p extensions/channel-broker/tsconfig.json` - passed. - `pnpm tsgo:core:test` - passed. - `pnpm exec oxfmt --check src/plugin-sdk/channel-broker.ts src/plugin-sdk/channel-broker.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/outbound.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/conformance.test.ts` - passed. - `pnpm exec oxlint extensions/channel-broker --format unix` - passed. - `git diff --check` - passed. ### Observed Result The SDK helpers normalize provider payloads without dropping native metadata, the bundled plugin refuses non-sent provider receipts as durable-send failures, and conformance tests prove the current broker path can represent the Phase 2 lifecycle requirements without changing user-visible channel defaults. ### Not Tested - Full local suite. Per `/Volumes/LEXAR` policy, broad validation is left to GitHub Actions. - Live Telegram, Discord, or Slack provider traffic. Those are intentionally scoped to Phase 2B/2C/2D. ## Current Stack Gate Context This branch is rebased on the current Phase 1 foundation head. Focused local validation is documented above or below; broad validation remains on GitHub Actions.

target.title: [Feature]: Channel Broker Phase 1 protocol foundation
target.body:
## Maintainer TL;DR This stack consolidates the recurring Telegram/Discord/Slack/WhatsApp/Signal/iMessage/etc. maintenance churn behind one Channel Broker contract. OpenClaw keeps the shared semantics that repeatedly regress across native channel plugins: sessions, allowlists, routing, /verbose, streaming policy, durable final sends, receipts, retries, and auditability. Broker providers own platform mechanics: bot APIs, bridge/device state, native ids, delivery quirks, and provider capabilities. This PR is the narrow foundation. It creates the versioned broker SDK subpath and bundled channel-broker plugin, but it does not move Telegram, Discord, Slack, or any user defaults. The direction is intentionally incremental: first land the versioned broker SDK/plugin foundation, then prove conformance, then prove opt-in routing for the major channels, then model long-tail and constrained providers. Only after migration paths exist should legacy native-channel feature work freeze in #86115. ## Why This Is Better Today, one fix to streaming, /verbose, or delivery behavior can make Telegram fast this week and then break Discord, Slack, or another native plugin the next week. The broker path makes those shared semantics one OpenClaw-owned contract instead of many platform-specific re-implementations. When a provider adds support for previews, threads, receipts, media, or constrained account modes, that capability can become available through the same contract to routed channels whose provider declares and passes conformance for it. ## Stack Shape ```mermaid flowchart TD U["Umbrella issue #86105: reduce maintained-channel churn"] --> P1["#86096 Phase 1: SDK + bundled broker foundation"] P1 --> P2A["#86153 Phase 2A: conformance + signed inbound"] P2A --> P2B["#86154 Phase 2B: Telegram opt-in proof"] P2B --> P2C["#86156 Phase 2C: Discord opt-in proof"] P2C --> P2D["#86157 Phase 2D: Slack opt-in proof"] P2D --> P3["#86164 Phase 3: official/app capability matrix"] P3 --> P4["#86165 Phase 4: constrained provider capabilities"] P4 -. "future, not in this stack" .-> P5["Future freeze issue #86115: legacy native-plugin freeze"] ``` | PR | Role in the consolidation | | --- | --- | | #86096 | Creates the versioned broker SDK/plugin foundation, provider routing guard, media final-send mapping, SecretRef/cancellation handling, and prefix-ownership contract. | | #86153 | Proves shared lifecycle conformance and adds signed inbound webhook registration/runtime handoff: inbound dedupe, durable final sends, receipts, retryable failures, preview/progress gates, and /verbose channelData. | | #86154 | Proves Telegram topics can route through broker targets without native Telegram internals. | | #86156 | Proves Discord DMs/channels/threads preserve conversation semantics through broker routing. | | #86157 | Proves Slack DMs/channel threads preserve direct/thread semantics through broker routing. | | #86164 | Adds official/app platform aliases, capability matrix rows, and declared delivery capability enforcement for long-tail channels. | | #86165 | Adds constrained-provider metadata for WhatsApp, Signal, iMessage, and bridge-backed providers. | ## Ownership Model ```mermaid flowchart LR Core["OpenClaw core
sessions, routing, allowlists
/verbose, streaming, retries
receipts, audit"] --> Broker["channel-broker plugin
openclaw/plugin-sdk/channel-broker
V1 protocol"] Broker --> Provider["Provider-owned broker
HTTP/webhook transport
platform adapters"] Provider --> Platforms["Telegram, Discord, Slack
Teams, Matrix, WhatsApp
Signal, iMessage, long tail"] Provider --> Broker Broker --> Core ``` ## Review Shape These PRs are intentionally linear. GitHub still shows `main` as the base for each PR so maintainers can land them one by one from fork branches; the branches themselves are stacked, and maintainers can review the focused adjacent diffs here: | PR | Focused adjacent diff | | --- | --- | | #86153 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-protocol...feat/channel-broker-conformance | | #86154 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-conformance...feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing | | #86156 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing...feat/channel-broker-discord-routing | | #86157 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-discord-routing...feat/channel-broker-slack-routing | | #86164 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-slack-routing...feat/channel-broker-official-platform-capabilities | | #86165 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-official-platform-capabilities...feat/channel-broker-constrained-platform-capabilities | ## This PR - Phase: Phase 1 - SDK and bundled broker foundation - Role: Creates the versioned broker SDK/plugin foundation, provider routing guard, media final-send mapping, SecretRef/cancellation handling, and prefix-ownership contract. - Head: `9e55f11ef9` ## Current Review State This PR is live for maintainer review. Current head: `9e55f11ef9`. Focused local validation for this phase is documented below. Broad validation remains owned by GitHub Actions. Native channel defaults remain unchanged unless this phase explicitly says otherwise. ## Summary Adds Phase 1 of the Channel Broker consolidation program: a versioned `openclaw/plugin-sdk/channel-broker` protocol surface, a bundled `channel-broker` channel plugin, provider-owned outbound request/receipt helpers, docs, and focused tests. This PR intentionally stays at the protocol/foundation layer. Telegram, Discord, Slack, and later channel migrations are tracked separately and should not be folded into this PR. ## Linked Context Closes #86107 Related #86105 Related #86108 Related #86109 Related #86110 Related #86111 Related #86112 Related #86113 Related #86114 Related #86115 ## Review Repair History The current Phase 1 branch includes focused repairs for ClawSweeper's blocker classes: - Preserves native prefix ownership when `channel-broker` declares platform aliases that overlap native plugins. If Telegram/Discord/Slack are registered, their prefixes stay native-owned; broker aliases own a platform prefix only when no native plugin declares it. - Adds the standard channel SecretRef contract for `channels.channel-broker.{outboundToken,signingSecret}` plus `accounts.*` and `providers.*` variants, and changes the broker schema/docs to use SecretRef-compatible credential input. - Threads `AbortSignal` through broker outbound requests, including the HTTP `fetch` transport and custom runtime override path. ## Real Behavior Proof - **Behavior or issue addressed:** Verified the Phase 1 broker surface resolves as a bundled channel and produces a normalized V1 outbound request for a Telegram-scoped broker target. This proves the user-visible foundation being shipped in this PR: catalog discovery, channel id normalization, target construction, protocol versioning, platform normalization, thread preservation, and final-send payload shape. - **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout at `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`, branch `feat/channel-broker-protocol`, after the current Phase 1 repair/doc series through `d39d47b3db`. - **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** ```bash node --import tsx -e 'const { listBundledChannelCatalogEntries } = await import("./src/channels/bundled-channel-catalog-read.ts"); const { normalizeChatChannelId } = await import("./src/channels/ids.ts"); const { buildBrokerConversationTarget, createBrokerOutboundRequest } = await import("./src/plugin-sdk/channel-broker.ts"); const catalog = listBundledChannelCatalogEntries().find((entry) => entry.id === "channel-broker"); const target = buildBrokerConversationTarget({ platform: "Telegram", conversationId: "chat 123", threadId: "topic-7" }); const request = createBrokerOutboundRequest({ requestId: "send-proof-1", providerId: "synthetic-provider", platform: "Telegram", accountId: "default", conversation: { id: "chat 123", type: "channel", threadId: "topic-7" }, mode: "final", payloads: [{ text: "broker proof" }], requirements: { text: true, thread: true } }); console.log(JSON.stringify({ discoveredChannel: catalog && { id: catalog.id, order: catalog.order, docsPath: catalog.channel.docsPath }, channelNormalization: normalizeChatChannelId("channel-broker"), target, outboundProtocolVersion: request.version, outboundMode: request.mode, outboundPlatform: request.platform, outboundConversation: request.conversation, outboundPayloadText: request.payloads[0]?.text }, null, 2));' ``` - **Evidence after fix:** Terminal output copied from the command above: ```json { "discoveredChannel": { "id": "channel-broker", "order": 60, "docsPath": "/channels/channel-broker" }, "channelNormalization": "channel-broker", "target": "telegram:chat%20123?threadId=topic-7", "outboundProtocolVersion": 1, "outboundMode": "final", "outboundPlatform": "telegram", "outboundConversation": { "id": "chat 123", "type": "channel", "threadId": "topic-7" }, "outboundPayloadText": "broker proof" } ``` - **Observed result after fix:** OpenClaw discovered the bundled `channel-broker` entry, normalized the channel id, built a Telegram broker target that preserved `threadId`, and emitted a V1 final outbound request with normalized `platform: "telegram"`, `conversation.threadId: "topic-7"`, and payload text `broker proof`. - **What was not tested:** No real third-party broker provider delivery was tested in Phase 1. That is intentionally deferred to the Phase 2 migration proof issues for Telegram, Discord, and Slack. ## Focused Local Validation All local validation below ran from `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`: ```bash git diff --check ``` ```bash node node_modules/oxfmt/bin/oxfmt --check --threads=1 --config .oxfmtrc.jsonc docs/channels/channel-broker.md docs/reference/secretref-credential-surface.md extensions/channel-broker/src/accounts.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/config-schema.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/outbound.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/runtime.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/secret-contract.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/secret-contract.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/types.ts src/infra/outbound/channel-target-prefix.ts src/infra/outbound/channel-target-prefix.test.ts ``` ```bash pnpm exec vitest run extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/secret-contract.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-channel-broker.config.ts ``` ```bash pnpm exec vitest run src/infra/outbound/channel-target-prefix.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.config.ts ``` ```bash pnpm exec tsc --noEmit -p extensions/channel-broker/tsconfig.json ``` ```bash pnpm exec vitest run test/scripts/bundled-plugin-build-entries.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.config.ts ``` ## Known Inherited Gates - `pnpm plugin-sdk:surface:check` has an inherited clean-upstream SDK budget drift; this PR adds the intended `plugin-sdk/channel-broker` public subpath. - `pnpm docs:check-i18n-glossary` has inherited unrelated glossary-label failures on clean upstream; Channel Broker glossary entries were added in this branch.

target.title: fix(gateway): clear admin scopes for backend self-pairing
target.body:
Summary
- Stop treating shared token/password auth as sufficient to preserve any self-declared operator scopes for local backend self-pairing.
- Keep unscoped backend local handoff intact, while routing scoped local backend Gateway calls through normal device identity so internal loopback calls still work.
- Add explicit issue coverage for loopback/no-Origin backend identity + shared token/password + self-declared operator scopes + no device identity.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** Device-less local/shared-secret backend Gateway clients must not preserve self-declared operator scopes while skipping pairing.

**Real environment tested:** Temporary local OpenClaw Gateway from this PR branch, token auth, loopback WebSocket at `ws://127.0.0.1:39139`, channels skipped, configured via a temporary `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR` and `OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH`.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Started the local Gateway from this branch, then ran a Node/tsx WebSocket client using `GatewayClient` as `gateway-client` / `backend` with shared token auth, `deviceIdentity: null`, and `scopes: ["operator.write"]`. The client then attempted `sessions.list`.

**Evidence after fix:** Terminal output from the live local Gateway client:

```text
$ OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/tmp/openclaw-pr86192-proof-... \
  OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/tmp/openclaw-pr86192-proof-.../openclaw.json \
  OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_PORT=39139 \
  OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_TOKEN=secret \
  node --import tsx --input-type=module

local gateway: ws://127.0.0.1:39139
backend shared-token no-device requested scopes: [operator.write]
hello auth scopes: []
sessions.list error: missing scope: operator.read
```

Gateway runtime log excerpt for the same live connection:

```text
[ws] ⇄ res ✗ sessions.list 1ms errorCode=INVALID_REQUEST errorMessage=missing scope: operator.read
```

**Observed result after fix:** The backend shared-token, no-device connection completed, but the server returned no authorized scopes in `hello-ok`; the follow-up read-scoped RPC failed with `missing scope: operator.read`. This verifies that non-admin self-declared scopes are cleared too, not only `operator.admin`.

**What was not tested:** Remote browser-origin behavior was not live-tested here; focused regression coverage covers the local and shared-secret-loopback cases changed by this PR.

Additional verification
```text
$ pnpm exec vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway.config.ts src/gateway/call.test.ts src/gateway/server/ws-connection/handshake-auth-helpers.test.ts src/gateway/server.auth.compat-baseline.test.ts src/gateway/server.auth.control-ui.test.ts

Test Files  4 passed (4)
Tests  165 passed (165)

$ pnpm exec vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway-server.config.ts src/gateway/server.sessions-send.test.ts

Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests  3 passed (3)

$ pnpm exec vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents.config.ts src/agents/code-mode.test.ts

Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests  30 passed (30)

$ git diff --check
# no output
```

Fixes openclaw/openclaw#72418

target.title: fix(cli): preserve Discord voice outbound helper
target.body:
Summary
- Keep CLI lazy outbound dependency routing from treating Discord voice helper keys as normal channel adapters.
- Preserve explicitly supplied `discordVoice` / `sendDiscordVoice` helpers while leaving missing helpers unresolved so the Discord runtime can decide the voice path.
- Add focused regression coverage for the CLI deps proxy and outbound send mapping, plus a changelog fix entry for #84952.

Verification
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cli/deps.test.ts src/cli/outbound-send-mapping.test.ts extensions/discord/src/outbound-adapter.test.ts src/cron/isolated-agent/delivery-dispatch.double-announce.test.ts`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check src/cli/deps.ts src/cli/deps.test.ts src/cli/outbound-send-mapping.ts src/cli/outbound-send-mapping.test.ts`
- `pnpm exec oxlint src/cli/deps.ts src/cli/deps.test.ts src/cli/outbound-send-mapping.ts src/cli/outbound-send-mapping.test.ts`
- `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --parallel-tests "node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cli/deps.test.ts src/cli/outbound-send-mapping.test.ts extensions/discord/src/outbound-adapter.test.ts src/cron/isolated-agent/delivery-dispatch.double-announce.test.ts"`
- `git diff --check`

Behavior addressed: Discord cron announces with `messages.tts.auto="always"` could fail before the Discord voice-message helper ran because the CLI outbound deps proxy manufactured a fake channel adapter for the `discordVoice` helper lookup.

Real environment tested: WSL2 Ubuntu source checkout on Node 22 with focused CLI, Discord outbound adapter, and cron double-announce regression tests. Crabbox/live Discord delivery was not run for this source-level fix.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cli/deps.test.ts src/cli/outbound-send-mapping.test.ts extensions/discord/src/outbound-adapter.test.ts src/cron/isolated-agent/delivery-dispatch.double-announce.test.ts`

Evidence after fix: The new CLI deps regression asserts `createDefaultDeps().discordVoice` and `createDefaultDeps().sendDiscordVoice` remain `undefined` instead of becoming unavailable channel adapters, and the outbound mapping regression asserts lazy channel factories are not called for those helper keys while explicit helpers are still preserved.

Observed result after fix: Focused regression suite passed: 4 files, 106 tests passed. Formatting, lint, diff check, and autoreview also passed.

What was not tested: A live Discord cron announce with generated TTS audio was not run before opening this PR; the proof is focused source-level regression coverage for the broken dependency lookup path.

Fixes #84952

target.title: fix(auth): point Keychain-only legacy Codex OAuth users at doctor instead of auto-prompting
target.body:
## Summary

Alternative to #85163 closing the same bucket from #85083 (macOS users whose legacy `oauthRef` sidecar seed lives only in the Keychain), but without an auto-prompt startup hook.

#85163 reuses the existing `maybeRepairLegacyOAuthSidecarProfiles` doctor flow as a pre-command hook in `runCli`, so every non-skipped interactive `openclaw` invocation on darwin can fire a Keychain migration prompt. That works, but trades a wide and growing skip surface (five ClawSweeper rounds, `--non-interactive`/`--yes`/`--no-input`/`--force`/`--json`/`channels remove --delete` and counting) for the auto-heal property — and it violates the AGENTS.md rule:

> Legacy config repair belongs in `openclaw doctor --fix`, not startup/load-time core migrations.

This PR takes the inverse design: keep doctor as the only place that migrates, and make sure affected headless users see a single, actionable error pointing at it instead of a silent fall-through to `No API key found for provider "openai-codex"`.

## Approach

Three concrete pieces, ~40 lines net:

1. **One-shot headless warn in `src/agents/auth-profiles/legacy-oauth-sidecar.ts`** — when `loadLegacyOAuthSidecarMaterial` returns null on darwin with `allowKeychainPrompt: false` (i.e., env/file seeds were tried and Keychain was blocked), emit a single `log.warn` per process naming `openclaw doctor --fix` and macOS Keychain. Rate-limited so embedded turns log it once per gateway lifetime, not per attempt. Guarded by the same VITEST env signals as the keychain read so unrelated test runs don't surface it.
2. **Wording cleanup in `src/commands/doctor-auth-oauth-sidecar.ts`** — drop the user-confusing `sidecar-backed` phrasing from the doctor prompt, notes, and changes (users don't know what a sidecar is); internal identifier names are left intact.
3. **Docs/changelog** — update `docs/gateway/doctor.md` accordion 5 to mention the new headless warning alongside the existing doctor-driven migration; add a single CHANGELOG bullet.

## What this deliberately doesn't do

- **No `runCli` pre-command hook.** No new file in `src/cli/`.
- **No interactive prompt outside `openclaw doctor --fix`.** Users who upgrade into the Keychain-only state see one warning telling them what to run; doctor remains the single place that prompts.
- **No decline marker, no env opt-out (`OPENCLAW_AUTO_MIGRATE_LEGACY_OAUTH_SIDECAR`), no skip ladder.** Nothing to bypass because nothing prompts.
- **No change to `maybeRepairLegacyOAuthSidecarProfiles` semantics.** Doctor still migrates exactly the same way it has since #83312.

## Trade vs. #85163

| | #85163 (auto-prompt hook) | This PR (fail-fast pointer) |
|---|---|---|
| User has to know about `openclaw doctor --fix` | No — prompt fires on next interactive command | Yes — one log line tells them |
| Surface where prompts can appear | Any non-skipped CLI command on darwin | None (only `openclaw doctor --fix`) |
| Skip-rule maintenance | Growing list (`channels remove --delete` was the latest) | None |
| `openclaw status` can surprise-prompt the user | Yes | No |
| Violates `AGENTS.md` "Legacy config repair belongs in doctor" | Yes (intentionally) | No |
| LOC added | +860 / -10 / 10 files | +183 / -10 / 7 files |

The cost of this design is honesty: we tell affected users they have one command to run instead of trying to auto-heal them silently. That cost is bounded — the headless warn is loud, actionable, and survives across restarts; `openclaw doctor --fix` is the one-line remediation. The benefit is the entire ClawSweeper skip-rule treadmill plus the AGENTS.md tension go away.

## Regression tests

- `src/agents/auth-profiles/legacy-oauth-sidecar.test.ts` (new) — exercises the headless warn path: emit once when blocked on darwin with no env/file seeds; never emit on non-darwin; rate-limited per process.

## Verification

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/auth-profiles/legacy-oauth-sidecar.test.ts src/commands/doctor-auth-oauth-sidecar.test.ts src/commands/doctor/repair-sequencing.test.ts` — 21/21 green (4 files; one is a co-resident sibling).
- `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs` clean on all touched files; `oxfmt --check` clean.

## Related

- Alternative to #85163
- Closes #85083 (same bucket, different shape)
- Companion to #85074 (file/env-seed bucket) and #85028 (fail-fast on missing refresh token)
- Original surface: #84893 / #84752 / #83312

target.title: fix: recover Codex binding after stale preflight compaction
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #86211.

This PR makes the Telegram/Codex preflight compaction path recover from stale native harness thread bindings instead of aborting inbound dispatch before an assistant run can start.

Changes:
- treats structured stale_thread_binding / missing_thread_binding native harness compaction failures as recoverable during preflight compaction
- keeps non-binding compaction failures fail-closed and visible
- lets Codex binding recovery create a fresh app-server thread when the old binding has already been cleared
- adds focused regression coverage for both the preflight dispatch path and the cleared-binding Codex recovery path

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: Inbound Telegram/Codex dispatch no longer aborts when preflight compaction sees a stale or missing Codex app-server thread binding. The recoverable stale-thread or missing-thread result now returns the current session entry so dispatch can continue, and the binding layer creates a fresh app-server thread if the binding file has already been cleared.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout at /root/.openclaw/workspace/openclaw-upstream on Linux with Node 22.22.2, branch fix-86211-missing-thread-preflight, commit 4b8ff8685fd9d4d3bcd901c30abc76ce9c98dd34, rebased on current origin/main.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran targeted OpenClaw runtime checks in the local checkout: node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.auto-reply-reply.config.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-memory.test.ts; node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.extensions.config.ts extensions/codex/src/conversation-binding.test.ts; node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.extensions.config.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/compact.test.ts; node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents-support.config.ts src/agents/command/cli-compaction.test.ts; focused npx oxlint on the changed core and extension files.

Evidence after fix: Local terminal output showed agent-runner-memory.test.ts passed with 27 tests, conversation-binding.test.ts passed with 13 tests, compact.test.ts passed with 26 tests, cli-compaction.test.ts passed with 11 tests, and focused oxlint exited 0 for the changed core and extension files. The command output was captured after rebasing on origin/main; PR text uses only redacted placeholders such as <codex-thread-id> and no chat IDs.

Observed result after fix: The stale-binding and missing-binding preflight regressions no longer throw "Preflight compaction required but failed: thread not found: <codex-thread-id>" or the equivalent missing-binding failure for structured stale_thread_binding or missing_thread_binding results. Non-binding failures still throw. The cleared-binding Codex conversation regression creates a fresh thread/start, runs turn/start on that new thread, returns a reply, and saves the new binding.

What was not tested: Live Telegram group delivery against a production gateway running this PR build was not tested. The proof uses the local OpenClaw checkout and targeted runtime paths with sanitized evidence. I requested the repository Mantis Telegram proof path in PR comment https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/86216#issuecomment-4530350483, but direct workflow dispatch requires maintainer/admin rights.

## Validation

Passed locally:
- node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.auto-reply-reply.config.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-memory.test.ts
- node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.extensions.config.ts extensions/codex/src/conversation-binding.test.ts
- node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.extensions.config.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/compact.test.ts
- node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents-support.config.ts src/agents/command/cli-compaction.test.ts
- npx oxlint --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.core.json src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-memory.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-memory.test.ts
- npx oxlint --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.extensions.json extensions/codex/src/conversation-binding.ts extensions/codex/src/conversation-binding.test.ts

Also ran node scripts/check-changed.mjs. Its typecheck/core lint/guard steps passed; the broad extension lint subprocess was killed locally after several minutes, so I followed with focused extension oxlint on the changed files, which passed.

## OpenClaw proof gate status

The repository OpenClaw `Real behavior proof` workflow has now re-evaluated the current PR head and passed.

- Head: `4b8ff8685fd9d4d3bcd901c30abc76ce9c98dd34`
- Run: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/actions/runs/26375423624
- Job log result: `External PR includes after-fix real behavior proof.`

This means the remaining status is no longer a contributor-side Mantis dispatch blocker. The earlier Mantis request is kept only as historical context for the previous proof suggestion; it is not the active unblock path.

A reviewer can now re-check the current PR state against the passing OpenClaw proof gate and the focused validation above.

## Privacy

All issue and PR evidence is sanitized. Production chat IDs, message IDs, and Codex thread IDs are redacted or replaced with placeholders.

target.title: fix(auth): auto-migrate keychain-only legacy Codex OAuth profiles on first interactive CLI run
target.body:
## Summary

Closes the remaining bucket from #85083: macOS users whose legacy `oauthRef` sidecar seed lives only in the Keychain still had to know to run `openclaw doctor --fix` after upgrade, because the embedded-runtime self-heal that landed in #85074 intentionally runs with `allowKeychainPrompt: false`.

The fix adds a tiny pre-command trigger in `runCli` (`src/cli/auto-migrate-legacy-oauth-sidecar.ts`) that, on darwin + TTY only, **silently** reuses the existing `maybeRepairLegacyOAuthSidecarProfiles` doctor flow when a sidecar dir is present. **There is no in-CLI confirm prompt — the macOS Keychain dialog is the only consent surface.** On Allow: migration completes inline and the original command proceeds. On Deny or any decryption failure: a permanent decline marker (`OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR/legacy-oauth-sidecar-migration-declined`) is written so the next CLI run does not re-trigger the OS dialog; `openclaw doctor --fix` is the explicit retry path. `OPENCLAW_AUTO_MIGRATE_LEGACY_OAUTH_SIDECAR=0` is an explicit opt-out for power users.

Headless paths (cron, systemd, Telegram polling, embedded sub-agent dispatch) still skip the auto-heal entirely — but the legacy sidecar loader now emits a one-shot `log.warn` that names `openclaw doctor --fix` and macOS Keychain, instead of letting the credential silently fall through to a downstream `No API key found for provider "openai-codex"`.

Also drops the user-confusing `sidecar-backed` phrasing from the doctor prompt/notes/changes; existing internal identifier names are left intact.

## Approach

Approach **B** from the issue (auto-trigger doctor migration on first interactive `openclaw <anything>` invocation), but **silent**: no clack confirm, no two-step "we're about to prompt you for Keychain access" UX. The macOS OS dialog itself is the explicit consent surface — clicking Allow once both authorizes the binary and triggers migration in the same step. Architecturally this leaves "when is it safe to prompt for Keychain access?" as one decision in one place (the embedded runtime path stays headless; the CLI path lets the OS prompt).

Skip rules in order: `process.platform !== "darwin"` → `!isInteractiveTty` (no Keychain dialog on cron/headless) → `CI=true || OPENCLAW_NON_INTERACTIVE=1 || OPENCLAW_AUTO_MIGRATE_LEGACY_OAUTH_SIDECAR=0 || OPENCLAW_AUTH_STORE_READONLY=1` → `--help/--version` → primary is `doctor`/`update`/`help`/`completion`/`version` → `--json`/`--json=…` (anywhere in argv before `--`, keeps clean output) → sidecar dir empty → fresh decline marker. The hook is dynamically imported only on darwin (gated at the call site in `runCli`).

**Skip rules deliberately removed from earlier revisions:** the auto-prompt design had a growing skip list for `--non-interactive`/`--yes`/`--no-input`/`--force` and `channels remove --delete` — the entire treadmill was about avoiding a clack prompt that no longer exists. With the OS Keychain dialog as the only consent surface, those flag-based skips are unnecessary; the OS dialog appears at most once per machine (Allow → never again; Deny → marker → never again).

## Headless one-shot warning

Added in `legacy-oauth-sidecar.ts`. Fires when `loadLegacyOAuthSidecarMaterial` returns null on darwin with `allowKeychainPrompt: false` (i.e., env/file seeds were tried and Keychain was blocked). Rate-limited to one warn per process so embedded turns log it once per gateway lifetime, not per attempt. Guarded by the same VITEST env signals as the keychain read so unrelated test runs don't surface it.

## Why this is safe vs. existing inline credentials

- **Re-signed-in profiles are invisible to the migration.** `writeOAuthCredentials` (`provider-auth-helpers.ts:297`) builds a fresh credential object with no `oauthRef`, and `upsertAuthProfile` (`profiles.ts:139`) does `store.profiles[id] = credential` — full replacement, not merge. So any user who re-signed in via normal flow has no `oauthRef` on their profile, and the migration scan (`resolveLegacyOAuthSidecarStore`, `doctor-auth-oauth-sidecar.ts:117-126`) only includes profiles where `value.oauthRef` is a valid `LegacyOAuthRef`. Migration is a no-op for them.
- **Runtime resolution already prefers inline material.** `persisted.ts:274-279` checks `hasInlineOAuthTokenMaterial` first; if inline tokens are present, the legacy sidecar resolver is never called regardless of what `oauthRef` says.
- **Sidecar credentials are real OAuth tokens.** Migration just moves them from "encrypted file referenced via `oauthRef`" to "inline access/refresh/idToken." Same values, same refresh cycle. If the refresh token still works → user is healed. If it's already revoked → user re-signs in via normal flow; migration never makes auth worse.

## Regression tests

- `src/cli/auto-migrate-legacy-oauth-sidecar.test.ts` — table-driven preflight skip checks for each rule (platform/TTY/env opt-outs/CI/readonly/no-sidecar/primary skips/`--json` variants), plus the silent happy path (sidecar removed, inline tokens written, no marker), unreferenced-sidecar bail-out, and decryption-failure → marker → second-run-skips. Asserts behavior (file state), not internal prompter calls.
- `src/agents/auth-profiles/legacy-oauth-sidecar.test.ts` — exercises the headless warn path: emit once when blocked on darwin with no env/file seeds; never emit on non-darwin.

## Verification

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cli/auto-migrate-legacy-oauth-sidecar.test.ts src/agents/auth-profiles/legacy-oauth-sidecar.test.ts src/commands/doctor-auth-oauth-sidecar.test.ts src/commands/doctor/repair-sequencing.test.ts` — 53/53 green across 5 files.
- `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs` clean on all touched files; `oxfmt --check` clean.

## Scope notes

- Did not touch `agents.add.test.ts` / `stale-oauth-profile-shadows.test.ts` test descriptions that still contain `sidecar-backed`; those are dev-facing and reference implementation, not UI copy.
- Did not externalize `OPENCLAW_AUTO_MIGRATE_LEGACY_OAUTH_SIDECAR` to docs anywhere except `docs/gateway/doctor.md` accordion 5 since it is an escape hatch, not a normal-user knob.
- No changes to `maybeRepairLegacyOAuthSidecarProfiles` itself beyond the wording rename — the migration semantics are intentionally identical between the doctor-driven and CLI-startup-driven paths. The CLI-startup caller passes `emitNotes: false` and a `confirmAutoFix: async () => true` prompter so the doctor function runs silently.

## Out of scope

- Removing the legacy sidecar code paths entirely. Tracked separately and should wait for the long tail to migrate.
- Changes to `openclaw doctor --fix` behavior itself; the migration path it runs is already correct.

## Related

- Closes #85083
- Companion to #85074 (file/env-seed bucket) and #85028 (fail-fast on missing refresh token)
- Original surface: #84893 / #84752 / #83312
- Alternative shape: #86220 (no auto-heal at all — fail-fast pointer to doctor only)

target.title: [Feature]: Channel Broker Phase 2B Telegram migration proof
target.body:
## Maintainer TL;DR This stack consolidates the recurring Telegram/Discord/Slack/WhatsApp/Signal/iMessage/etc. maintenance churn behind one Channel Broker contract. OpenClaw keeps the shared semantics that repeatedly regress across native channel plugins: sessions, allowlists, routing, /verbose, streaming policy, durable final sends, receipts, retries, and auditability. Broker providers own platform mechanics: bot APIs, bridge/device state, native ids, delivery quirks, and provider capabilities. This PR proves Telegram topics can move through broker-owned target parsing without changing native Telegram defaults. It is intentionally an opt-in proof layer, not the default migration. The direction is intentionally incremental: first land the versioned broker SDK/plugin foundation, then prove conformance, then prove opt-in routing for the major channels, then model long-tail and constrained providers. Only after migration paths exist should legacy native-channel feature work freeze in #86115. ## Why This Is Better Today, one fix to streaming, /verbose, or delivery behavior can make Telegram fast this week and then break Discord, Slack, or another native plugin the next week. The broker path makes those shared semantics one OpenClaw-owned contract instead of many platform-specific re-implementations. When a provider adds support for previews, threads, receipts, media, or constrained account modes, that capability can become available through the same contract to routed channels whose provider declares and passes conformance for it. ## Stack Shape ```mermaid flowchart TD U["Umbrella issue #86105: reduce maintained-channel churn"] --> P1["#86096 Phase 1: SDK + bundled broker foundation"] P1 --> P2A["#86153 Phase 2A: conformance + signed inbound"] P2A --> P2B["#86154 Phase 2B: Telegram opt-in proof"] P2B --> P2C["#86156 Phase 2C: Discord opt-in proof"] P2C --> P2D["#86157 Phase 2D: Slack opt-in proof"] P2D --> P3["#86164 Phase 3: official/app capability matrix"] P3 --> P4["#86165 Phase 4: constrained provider capabilities"] P4 -. "future, not in this stack" .-> P5["Future freeze issue #86115: legacy native-plugin freeze"] ``` | PR | Role in the consolidation | | --- | --- | | #86096 | Creates the versioned broker SDK/plugin foundation, provider routing guard, media final-send mapping, SecretRef/cancellation handling, and prefix-ownership contract. | | #86153 | Proves shared lifecycle conformance and adds signed inbound webhook registration/runtime handoff: inbound dedupe, durable final sends, receipts, retryable failures, preview/progress gates, and /verbose channelData. | | #86154 | Proves Telegram topics can route through broker targets without native Telegram internals. | | #86156 | Proves Discord DMs/channels/threads preserve conversation semantics through broker routing. | | #86157 | Proves Slack DMs/channel threads preserve direct/thread semantics through broker routing. | | #86164 | Adds official/app platform aliases, capability matrix rows, and declared delivery capability enforcement for long-tail channels. | | #86165 | Adds constrained-provider metadata for WhatsApp, Signal, iMessage, and bridge-backed providers. | ## Ownership Model ```mermaid flowchart LR Core["OpenClaw core
sessions, routing, allowlists
/verbose, streaming, retries
receipts, audit"] --> Broker["channel-broker plugin
openclaw/plugin-sdk/channel-broker
V1 protocol"] Broker --> Provider["Provider-owned broker
HTTP/webhook transport
platform adapters"] Provider --> Platforms["Telegram, Discord, Slack
Teams, Matrix, WhatsApp
Signal, iMessage, long tail"] Provider --> Broker Broker --> Core ``` ## Review Shape These PRs are intentionally linear. GitHub still shows `main` as the base for each PR so maintainers can land them one by one from fork branches; the branches themselves are stacked, and maintainers can review the focused adjacent diffs here: | PR | Focused adjacent diff | | --- | --- | | #86153 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-protocol...feat/channel-broker-conformance | | #86154 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-conformance...feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing | | #86156 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing...feat/channel-broker-discord-routing | | #86157 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-discord-routing...feat/channel-broker-slack-routing | | #86164 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-slack-routing...feat/channel-broker-official-platform-capabilities | | #86165 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-official-platform-capabilities...feat/channel-broker-constrained-platform-capabilities | ## This PR - Phase: Phase 2B - Telegram opt-in broker routing proof - Role: Proves Telegram topics can route through broker targets without native Telegram internals. - Head: `dbaca37c67` ## Current Review State This PR is live for maintainer review. Current head: `dbaca37c67`. Focused local validation for this phase is documented below. Broad validation remains owned by GitHub Actions. Native channel defaults remain unchanged unless this phase explicitly says otherwise. ## Summary - Adds the Phase 2B Telegram broker routing proof on top of #86153 and #86096. - Normalizes Telegram topic shorthand in the broker plugin, including `broker:telegram::topic:` and numeric `:` targets. - Canonicalizes outbound broker session routes to `telegram:?threadId=` so topic routing is stable without importing native Telegram internals. ## Rollout Context Closes #86110 Related #86108 Related #86105 Stacked on #86153 Stacked on #86096 Supersedes closed PR #86137, which was closed by the repository active-PR cap rather than by an implementation failure. Native Telegram defaults stay unchanged. Existing target-prefix ownership still keeps `telegram:` owned by the native Telegram plugin when that plugin is installed; this PR proves the opt-in broker route through `broker:telegram:...` and the broker alias path when native Telegram is absent. For review, the focused stack diff is: https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-conformance...feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing ## Real Behavior Proof - **Behavior or issue addressed:** Telegram topic targets can route through `channel-broker` and become provider requests with canonical `telegram` platform, channel conversation id, and topic `threadId`. - **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout at `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`, using the Channel Broker plugin and SDK from the stacked rollout branch on May 24, 2026. - **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran a `node --import tsx --input-type=module` terminal proof that called `channelBrokerPlugin.message.send.text` with `to: "broker:telegram:-1001234567890:topic:42"` and captured the provider request passed to the configured broker runtime. - **Evidence after fix:** Terminal output excerpt from that local command: ```json { "label": "telegram-topic", "messageId": "native-telegram-topic", "platform": "telegram", "conversation": { "id": "-1001234567890", "type": "channel", "threadId": "42" }, "requirements": { "text": true, "thread": true } } ``` - **Observed result after fix:** The live plugin path normalized Telegram topic shorthand into the broker provider request shape without importing native Telegram extension internals. - **What was not tested:** Live Telegram Bot API traffic and the full local suite; this PR stays scoped to opt-in broker routing proof. ### Behavior OpenClaw can route a Telegram topic through `channel-broker` without Telegram extension code. The broker receives a normalized platform `telegram`, conversation id, and thread id; OpenClaw keeps the session route canonical and leaves native Telegram prefix ownership intact. ### Environment - Local checkout: `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker` - Branch: `feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing` - Head: `dbaca37c67` (current Telegram proof head) ### Steps 1. Sent a synthetic message to `broker:telegram:-1001234567890:topic:42` through `channelBrokerPlugin.message.send.text`. 2. Verified the provider request used `platform: "telegram"`, `conversation.id: "-1001234567890"`, and `conversation.threadId: "42"`. 3. Resolved the outbound session route for the same target. 4. Verified the route canonicalized to `telegram:-1001234567890?threadId=42` and preserved channel chat semantics. ### Evidence - `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/conformance.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-channel-broker.config.ts` - 2 files passed, 10 tests passed. - `pnpm exec tsc --noEmit -p extensions/channel-broker/tsconfig.json` - passed. - `pnpm exec oxfmt --check extensions/channel-broker/src/target.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts` - passed. - `pnpm exec oxlint extensions/channel-broker --format unix` - passed. - `git diff --check` - passed. ### Observed Result Telegram topic routing is represented by broker-owned target normalization and provider request semantics, not by core importing or calling native Telegram extension internals. ### Not Tested - Live Telegram Bot API traffic; this PR is the opt-in broker migration proof, not a live provider connector. - Full local suite. Per `/Volumes/LEXAR` policy, broad validation is left to GitHub Actions. ## Current Stack Gate Context This branch is rebased on the current Phase 1 foundation head. Focused local validation is documented above or below; broad validation remains on GitHub Actions.

commands.author: omarshahine

target.title: Repair anchorless iMessage watch payload routing
target.body:
## Summary
- recover malformed anchorless iMessage watch payloads by GUID before debounce/routing
- drop unrecoverable anchorless payloads fail-closed instead of routing them to the sender DM
- add unit coverage for the repair/drop helper plus a monitor-level route assertion that the final dispatch target is the recovered group

## Why
A group link-preview watch payload can arrive with `chat_id=0`, empty conversation identifiers, and `is_group=false`. Without a pre-routing repair/drop guard, OpenClaw can treat the payload as a direct message and send the reply to `imessage:<sender>` instead of the originating group.

Closes #84470.
Refs #84503.

Credit: this follows the bug report and mitigation direction from @zqchris and the contributor work in #84503 by @zhangguiping-xydt, but reapplies the fix on current `main` with broader anchorless detection and route-level assertions.

## Verification
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/imessage/src/monitor/conversation-repair.test.ts extensions/imessage/src/monitor.last-route.test.ts extensions/imessage/src/monitor.watch-subscribe-retry.test.ts extensions/imessage/src/monitor.gating.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.extensions.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/extensions-test.tsbuildinfo`
- `pnpm lint:extensions:bundled`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check CHANGELOG.md extensions/imessage/src/monitor/conversation-repair.ts extensions/imessage/src/monitor/conversation-repair.test.ts extensions/imessage/src/monitor/monitor-provider.ts extensions/imessage/src/monitor.last-route.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`

## Live iMessage proof
Ran on the Lobster Mac against the live `imsg rpc` bridge from this PR branch (`c89bbe2862`). The probe used `chats.list` and `messages.history`, built a synthetic anchorless payload from a real group-history message, and called the same repair helper added by this PR.

Repair proof, redacted:

```json
{
  "ok": true,
  "sourceWasAnchorless": true,
  "repairedWasAnchorless": false,
  "groupCount": 3,
  "selectedChatId": 18,
  "repairedChatId": 18,
  "sourceGuid": "A3294...CCF2E5",
  "repairedChatGuid": "any;+...b7e6aa",
  "repairedIsGroup": true,
  "participantsRecovered": 8
}
```

Fail-closed proof with a non-existent anchorless GUID, redacted:

```json
{
  "ok": true,
  "sourceWasAnchorless": true,
  "result": "dropped",
  "diagnosticCount": 1
}
```

commands.author: jason-allen-oneal

target.title: fix(gateway): clear admin scopes for backend self-pairing
target.body:
Summary
- Stop treating shared token/password auth as sufficient to preserve any self-declared operator scopes for local backend self-pairing.
- Keep unscoped backend local handoff intact, while routing scoped local backend Gateway calls through normal device identity so internal loopback calls still work.
- Add explicit issue coverage for loopback/no-Origin backend identity + shared token/password + self-declared operator scopes + no device identity.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** Device-less local/shared-secret backend Gateway clients must not preserve self-declared operator scopes while skipping pairing.

**Real environment tested:** Temporary local OpenClaw Gateway from this PR branch, token auth, loopback WebSocket at `ws://127.0.0.1:39139`, channels skipped, configured via a temporary `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR` and `OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH`.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Started the local Gateway from this branch, then ran a Node/tsx WebSocket client using `GatewayClient` as `gateway-client` / `backend` with shared token auth, `deviceIdentity: null`, and `scopes: ["operator.write"]`. The client then attempted `sessions.list`.

**Evidence after fix:** Terminal output from the live local Gateway client:

```text
$ OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/tmp/openclaw-pr86192-proof-... \
  OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/tmp/openclaw-pr86192-proof-.../openclaw.json \
  OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_PORT=39139 \
  OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_TOKEN=secret \
  node --import tsx --input-type=module

local gateway: ws://127.0.0.1:39139
backend shared-token no-device requested scopes: [operator.write]
hello auth scopes: []
sessions.list error: missing scope: operator.read
```

Gateway runtime log excerpt for the same live connection:

```text
[ws] ⇄ res ✗ sessions.list 1ms errorCode=INVALID_REQUEST errorMessage=missing scope: operator.read
```

**Observed result after fix:** The backend shared-token, no-device connection completed, but the server returned no authorized scopes in `hello-ok`; the follow-up read-scoped RPC failed with `missing scope: operator.read`. This verifies that non-admin self-declared scopes are cleared too, not only `operator.admin`.

**What was not tested:** Remote browser-origin behavior was not live-tested here; focused regression coverage covers the local and shared-secret-loopback cases changed by this PR.

Additional verification
```text
$ pnpm exec vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway.config.ts src/gateway/call.test.ts src/gateway/server/ws-connection/handshake-auth-helpers.test.ts src/gateway/server.auth.compat-baseline.test.ts src/gateway/server.auth.control-ui.test.ts

Test Files  4 passed (4)
Tests  165 passed (165)

$ pnpm exec vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway-server.config.ts src/gateway/server.sessions-send.test.ts

Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests  3 passed (3)

$ pnpm exec vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents.config.ts src/agents/code-mode.test.ts

Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests  30 passed (30)

$ git diff --check
# no output
```

Fixes openclaw/openclaw#72418

It creates the versioned broker SDK subpath and bundled channel-broker plugin, but it does not move Telegram, Discord, Slack, or any user defaults. The direction is intentionally incremental: first land the versioned broker SDK/plugin foundation, then prove conformance, then prove opt-in routing for the major channels, then model long-tail and constrained providers. GitHub still shows `main` as the base for each PR so maintainers can land them one by one from fork branches; the branches themselves are stacked, and maintainers can review the focused adjacent diffs here: | PR | Focused adjacent diff | | --- | --- | | #86153 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-protocol...feat/channel-broker-conformance | | #86154 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-conformance...feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing | | #86156 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing...feat/channel-broker-discord-routing | | #86157 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-discord-routing...feat/channel-broker-slack-routing | | #86164 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-slack-routing...feat/channel-broker-official-platform-capabilities | | #86165 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-official-platform-capabilities...feat/channel-broker-constrained-platform-capabilities | ## This PR - Phase: Phase 1 - SDK and bundled broker foundation - Role: Creates the versioned broker SDK/plugin foundation, provider routing guard, media final-send mapping, SecretRef/cancellation handling, and prefix-ownership contract. - Head: `9e55f11ef9` ## Current Review State This PR is live for maintainer review. Current head: `9e55f11ef9`. Focused local validation for this phase is documented below. Broad validation remains owned by GitHub Actions. Native channel defaults remain unchanged unless this phase explicitly says otherwise. ## Summary Adds Phase 1 of the Channel Broker consolidation program: a versioned `openclaw/plugin-sdk/channel-broker` protocol surface, a bundled `channel-broker` channel plugin, provider-owned outbound request/receipt helpers, docs, and focused tests. This PR intentionally stays at the protocol/foundation layer. Telegram, Discord, Slack, and later channel migrations are tracked separately and should not be folded into this PR. ## Linked Context Closes #86107 Related #86105 Related #86108 Related #86109 Related #86110 Related #86111 Related #86112 Related #86113 Related #86114 Related #86115 ## Review Repair History The current Phase 1 branch includes focused repairs for ClawSweeper's blocker classes: - Preserves native prefix ownership when `channel-broker` declares platform aliases that overlap native plugins. If Telegram/Discord/Slack are registered, their prefixes stay native-owned; broker aliases own a platform prefix only when no native plugin declares it. - Adds the standard channel SecretRef contract for `channels.channel-broker.{outboundToken,signingSecret}` plus `accounts.*` and `providers.*` variants, and changes the broker schema/docs to use SecretRef-compatible credential input. - Threads `AbortSignal` through broker outbound requests, including the HTTP `fetch` transport and custom runtime override path. ## Real Behavior Proof - **Behavior or issue addressed:** Verified the Phase 1 broker surface resolves as a bundled channel and produces a normalized V1 outbound request for a Telegram-scoped broker target. This proves the user-visible foundation being shipped in this PR: catalog discovery, channel id normalization, target construction, protocol versioning, platform normalization, thread preservation, and final-send payload shape. - **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout at `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`, branch `feat/channel-broker-protocol`, after the current Phase 1 repair/doc series through `d39d47b3db`. - **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** ```bash node --import tsx -e 'const { listBundledChannelCatalogEntries } = await import("./src/channels/bundled-channel-catalog-read.ts"); const { normalizeChatChannelId } = await import("./src/channels/ids.ts"); const { buildBrokerConversationTarget, createBrokerOutboundRequest } = await import("./src/plugin-sdk/channel-broker.ts"); const catalog = listBundledChannelCatalogEntries().find((entry) => entry.id === "channel-broker"); const target = buildBrokerConversationTarget({ platform: "Telegram", conversationId: "chat 123", threadId: "topic-7" }); const request = createBrokerOutboundRequest({ requestId: "send-proof-1", providerId: "synthetic-provider", platform: "Telegram", accountId: "default", conversation: { id: "chat 123", type: "channel", threadId: "topic-7" }, mode: "final", payloads: [{ text: "broker proof" }], requirements: { text: true, thread: true } }); console.log(JSON.stringify({ discoveredChannel: catalog && { id: catalog.id, order: catalog.order, docsPath: catalog.channel.docsPath }, channelNormalization: normalizeChatChannelId("channel-broker"), target, outboundProtocolVersion: request.version, outboundMode: request.mode, outboundPlatform: request.platform, outboundConversation: request.conversation, outboundPayloadText: request.payloads[0]?.text }, null, 2));' ``` - **Evidence after fix:** Terminal output copied from the command above: ```json { "discoveredChannel": { "id": "channel-broker", "order": 60, "docsPath": "/channels/channel-broker" }, "channelNormalization": "channel-broker", "target": "telegram:chat%20123?threadId=topic-7", "outboundProtocolVersion": 1, "outboundMode": "final", "outboundPlatform": "telegram", "outboundConversation": { "id": "chat 123", "type": "channel", "threadId": "topic-7" }, "outboundPayloadText": "broker proof" } ``` - **Observed result after fix:** OpenClaw discovered the bundled `channel-broker` entry, normalized the channel id, built a Telegram broker target that preserved `threadId`, and emitted a V1 final outbound request with normalized `platform: "telegram"`, `conversation.threadId: "topic-7"`, and payload text `broker proof`. - **What was not tested:** No real third-party broker provider delivery was tested in Phase 1. That is intentionally deferred to the Phase 2 migration proof issues for Telegram, Discord, and Slack. ## Focused Local Validation All local validation below ran from `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`: ```bash git diff --check ``` ```bash node node_modules/oxfmt/bin/oxfmt --check --threads=1 --config .oxfmtrc.jsonc docs/channels/channel-broker.md docs/reference/secretref-credential-surface.md extensions/channel-broker/src/accounts.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/config-schema.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/outbound.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/runtime.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/secret-contract.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/secret-contract.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/types.ts src/infra/outbound/channel-target-prefix.ts src/infra/outbound/channel-target-prefix.test.ts ``` ```bash pnpm exec vitest run extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/secret-contract.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-channel-broker.config.ts ``` ```bash pnpm exec vitest run src/infra/outbound/channel-target-prefix.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.config.ts ``` ```bash pnpm exec tsc --noEmit -p extensions/channel-broker/tsconfig.json ``` ```bash pnpm exec vitest run test/scripts/bundled-plugin-build-entries.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.config.ts ``` ## Known Inherited Gates - `pnpm plugin-sdk:surface:check` has an inherited clean-upstream SDK budget drift; this PR adds the intended `plugin-sdk/channel-broker` public subpath. - `pnpm docs:check-i18n-glossary` has inherited unrelated glossary-label failures on clean upstream; Channel Broker glossary entries were added in this branch.

target.title: fix(google): remove stale -preview rewrite for gemini-3.1-flash-lite (GA since May 2026)
target.body:
## Summary

- `gemini-3.1-flash-lite` became GA on May 7, 2026. The `-preview` model is being shut down on May 25, 2026 (today).
- OpenClaw was still rewriting `gemini-3.1-flash-lite` → `gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview` in the normalization layer, causing all requests to route to the soon-to-be-dead preview endpoint.
- Removes the rewrite from the extension normalizer and the public SDK copy.
- Updates the `gemini-flash-lite` global alias and the Gemini CLI `flash-lite` alias to point at the GA model ID.
- Updates all affected tests.

## Test plan

- [x] `pnpm test extensions/google/model-id.test.ts extensions/google/api.test.ts` — 22 passed
- [x] `pnpm test src/config/model-alias-defaults.test.ts src/agents/models-config.providers.google-antigravity.test.ts` — 12 passed
- [x] `pnpm check` — clean

Fixes #86151

target.title: [Feature]: Channel Broker Phase 2D Slack migration proof
target.body:
## Maintainer TL;DR This stack consolidates the recurring Telegram/Discord/Slack/WhatsApp/Signal/iMessage/etc. maintenance churn behind one Channel Broker contract. OpenClaw keeps the shared semantics that repeatedly regress across native channel plugins: sessions, allowlists, routing, /verbose, streaming policy, durable final sends, receipts, retries, and auditability. Broker providers own platform mechanics: bot APIs, bridge/device state, native ids, delivery quirks, and provider capabilities. This PR proves Slack DMs and channel threads can route through broker-owned semantics while native Slack remains the default path. The direction is intentionally incremental: first land the versioned broker SDK/plugin foundation, then prove conformance, then prove opt-in routing for the major channels, then model long-tail and constrained providers. Only after migration paths exist should legacy native-channel feature work freeze in #86115. ## Why This Is Better Today, one fix to streaming, /verbose, or delivery behavior can make Telegram fast this week and then break Discord, Slack, or another native plugin the next week. The broker path makes those shared semantics one OpenClaw-owned contract instead of many platform-specific re-implementations. When a provider adds support for previews, threads, receipts, media, or constrained account modes, that capability can become available through the same contract to routed channels whose provider declares and passes conformance for it. ## Stack Shape ```mermaid flowchart TD U["Umbrella issue #86105: reduce maintained-channel churn"] --> P1["#86096 Phase 1: SDK + bundled broker foundation"] P1 --> P2A["#86153 Phase 2A: conformance + signed inbound"] P2A --> P2B["#86154 Phase 2B: Telegram opt-in proof"] P2B --> P2C["#86156 Phase 2C: Discord opt-in proof"] P2C --> P2D["#86157 Phase 2D: Slack opt-in proof"] P2D --> P3["#86164 Phase 3: official/app capability matrix"] P3 --> P4["#86165 Phase 4: constrained provider capabilities"] P4 -. "future, not in this stack" .-> P5["Future freeze issue #86115: legacy native-plugin freeze"] ``` | PR | Role in the consolidation | | --- | --- | | #86096 | Creates the versioned broker SDK/plugin foundation, provider routing guard, media final-send mapping, SecretRef/cancellation handling, and prefix-ownership contract. | | #86153 | Proves shared lifecycle conformance and adds signed inbound webhook registration/runtime handoff: inbound dedupe, durable final sends, receipts, retryable failures, preview/progress gates, and /verbose channelData. | | #86154 | Proves Telegram topics can route through broker targets without native Telegram internals. | | #86156 | Proves Discord DMs/channels/threads preserve conversation semantics through broker routing. | | #86157 | Proves Slack DMs/channel threads preserve direct/thread semantics through broker routing. | | #86164 | Adds official/app platform aliases, capability matrix rows, and declared delivery capability enforcement for long-tail channels. | | #86165 | Adds constrained-provider metadata for WhatsApp, Signal, iMessage, and bridge-backed providers. | ## Ownership Model ```mermaid flowchart LR Core["OpenClaw core
sessions, routing, allowlists
/verbose, streaming, retries
receipts, audit"] --> Broker["channel-broker plugin
openclaw/plugin-sdk/channel-broker
V1 protocol"] Broker --> Provider["Provider-owned broker
HTTP/webhook transport
platform adapters"] Provider --> Platforms["Telegram, Discord, Slack
Teams, Matrix, WhatsApp
Signal, iMessage, long tail"] Provider --> Broker Broker --> Core ``` ## Review Shape These PRs are intentionally linear. GitHub still shows `main` as the base for each PR so maintainers can land them one by one from fork branches; the branches themselves are stacked, and maintainers can review the focused adjacent diffs here: | PR | Focused adjacent diff | | --- | --- | | #86153 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-protocol...feat/channel-broker-conformance | | #86154 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-conformance...feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing | | #86156 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing...feat/channel-broker-discord-routing | | #86157 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-discord-routing...feat/channel-broker-slack-routing | | #86164 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-slack-routing...feat/channel-broker-official-platform-capabilities | | #86165 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-official-platform-capabilities...feat/channel-broker-constrained-platform-capabilities | ## This PR - Phase: Phase 2D - Slack opt-in broker routing proof - Role: Proves Slack DMs/channel threads preserve direct/thread semantics through broker routing. - Head: `cf14b47e31` ## Current Review State This PR is live for maintainer review. Current head: `cf14b47e31`. Focused local validation for this phase is documented below. Broad validation remains owned by GitHub Actions. Native channel defaults remain unchanged unless this phase explicitly says otherwise. ## Summary - Adds the Phase 2D Slack migration proof for the Channel Broker rollout. - Keeps native Slack default behavior unchanged; this only proves opt-in broker routing for Slack targets. - Stacks on Phase 2C Discord proof (#86156), Phase 2B Telegram proof (#86154), Phase 2A conformance baseline (#86153), and Phase 1 broker foundation (#86096). Closes #86112 Related #86108 Related #86105 Supersedes closed PR #86142, which was closed by the repository active-PR cap rather than by an implementation failure. ## What changed - Normalize broker-prefixed Slack DM targets such as `broker:slack:user:U12345678` into broker provider requests with `conversation.type = "direct"`. - Normalize broker-prefixed Slack channel thread targets such as `broker:slack:channel:C12345678?threadId=1716500000.000001` into broker provider requests with `conversation.type = "channel"` and `threadId`. - Canonicalize Slack broker session routes to the resolved platform target, preserving direct-route semantics as `slack:direct%3A` and thread routes as `slack:?threadId=`. ## Real Behavior Proof - **Behavior or issue addressed:** Slack DM and threaded-channel targets can route through `channel-broker` while preserving direct/channel conversation semantics and Slack `threadTs` as broker `threadId`. - **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout at `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`, using the Channel Broker plugin and SDK from the stacked rollout branch on May 24, 2026. - **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran a `node --import tsx --input-type=module` terminal proof that called `channelBrokerPlugin.message.send.text` with `to: "broker:slack:channel:C12345678?threadId=1716500000.000001"` and captured the provider request passed to the configured broker runtime. - **Evidence after fix:** Terminal output excerpt from that local command: ```json { "label": "slack-thread", "messageId": "native-slack-thread", "platform": "slack", "conversation": { "id": "C12345678", "type": "channel", "threadId": "1716500000.000001" }, "requirements": { "text": true, "thread": true } } ``` - **Observed result after fix:** The live plugin path produced the expected Slack provider request and kept threaded delivery represented by the broker `threadId` field. - **What was not tested:** Live Slack API traffic and the full local suite; this PR stays scoped to opt-in broker routing proof. Focused diff for this stack layer: https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-discord-routing...feat/channel-broker-slack-routing ## Validation Run locally from `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`: - `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/conformance.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-channel-broker.config.ts` - `pnpm exec tsc --noEmit -p extensions/channel-broker/tsconfig.json` - `git diff --check` - `pnpm exec oxfmt --check extensions/channel-broker/src/target.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts` - `pnpm exec oxlint extensions/channel-broker --format unix` ## Review state - Live for maintainer review; no longer draft. - No native Slack migration is enabled by default in this PR. - Includes a tiny test-isolation hardening for `src/agents/model-catalog-visibility.test.ts` after the unrelated `checks-node-agentic-agents` shard repeatedly timed out there while the single file passed locally.
