target.title: [Feature]: Channel Broker Phase 2D Slack migration proof
target.body:
## Maintainer TL;DR This stack consolidates the recurring Telegram/Discord/Slack/WhatsApp/Signal/iMessage/etc. maintenance churn behind one Channel Broker contract. OpenClaw keeps the shared semantics that repeatedly regress across native channel plugins: sessions, allowlists, routing, /verbose, streaming policy, durable final sends, receipts, retries, and auditability. Broker providers own platform mechanics: bot APIs, bridge/device state, native ids, delivery quirks, and provider capabilities. This PR proves Slack DMs and channel threads can route through broker-owned semantics while native Slack remains the default path. The direction is intentionally incremental: first land the versioned broker SDK/plugin foundation, then prove conformance, then prove opt-in routing for the major channels, then model long-tail and constrained providers. Only after migration paths exist should legacy native-channel feature work freeze in #86115. ## Why This Is Better Today, one fix to streaming, /verbose, or delivery behavior can make Telegram fast this week and then break Discord, Slack, or another native plugin the next week. The broker path makes those shared semantics one OpenClaw-owned contract instead of many platform-specific re-implementations. When a provider adds support for previews, threads, receipts, media, or constrained account modes, that capability can become available through the same contract to routed channels whose provider declares and passes conformance for it. ## Stack Shape ```mermaid flowchart TD U["Umbrella issue #86105: reduce maintained-channel churn"] --> P1["#86096 Phase 1: SDK + bundled broker foundation"] P1 --> P2A["#86153 Phase 2A: conformance + signed inbound"] P2A --> P2B["#86154 Phase 2B: Telegram opt-in proof"] P2B --> P2C["#86156 Phase 2C: Discord opt-in proof"] P2C --> P2D["#86157 Phase 2D: Slack opt-in proof"] P2D --> P3["#86164 Phase 3: official/app capability matrix"] P3 --> P4["#86165 Phase 4: constrained provider capabilities"] P4 -. "future, not in this stack" .-> P5["Future freeze issue #86115: legacy native-plugin freeze"] ``` | PR | Role in the consolidation | | --- | --- | | #86096 | Creates the versioned broker SDK/plugin foundation, provider routing guard, media final-send mapping, SecretRef/cancellation handling, and prefix-ownership contract. | | #86153 | Proves shared lifecycle conformance and adds signed inbound webhook registration/runtime handoff: inbound dedupe, durable final sends, receipts, retryable failures, preview/progress gates, and /verbose channelData. | | #86154 | Proves Telegram topics can route through broker targets without native Telegram internals. | | #86156 | Proves Discord DMs/channels/threads preserve conversation semantics through broker routing. | | #86157 | Proves Slack DMs/channel threads preserve direct/thread semantics through broker routing. | | #86164 | Adds official/app platform aliases, capability matrix rows, and declared delivery capability enforcement for long-tail channels. | | #86165 | Adds constrained-provider metadata for WhatsApp, Signal, iMessage, and bridge-backed providers. | ## Ownership Model ```mermaid flowchart LR Core["OpenClaw core
sessions, routing, allowlists
/verbose, streaming, retries
receipts, audit"] --> Broker["channel-broker plugin
openclaw/plugin-sdk/channel-broker
V1 protocol"] Broker --> Provider["Provider-owned broker
HTTP/webhook transport
platform adapters"] Provider --> Platforms["Telegram, Discord, Slack
Teams, Matrix, WhatsApp
Signal, iMessage, long tail"] Provider --> Broker Broker --> Core ``` ## Review Shape These PRs are intentionally linear. GitHub still shows `main` as the base for each PR so maintainers can land them one by one from fork branches; the branches themselves are stacked, and maintainers can review the focused adjacent diffs here: | PR | Focused adjacent diff | | --- | --- | | #86153 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-protocol...feat/channel-broker-conformance | | #86154 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-conformance...feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing | | #86156 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing...feat/channel-broker-discord-routing | | #86157 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-discord-routing...feat/channel-broker-slack-routing | | #86164 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-slack-routing...feat/channel-broker-official-platform-capabilities | | #86165 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-official-platform-capabilities...feat/channel-broker-constrained-platform-capabilities | ## This PR - Phase: Phase 2D - Slack opt-in broker routing proof - Role: Proves Slack DMs/channel threads preserve direct/thread semantics through broker routing. - Head: `cf14b47e31` ## Current Review State This PR is live for maintainer review. Current head: `cf14b47e31`. Focused local validation for this phase is documented below. Broad validation remains owned by GitHub Actions. Native channel defaults remain unchanged unless this phase explicitly says otherwise. ## Summary - Adds the Phase 2D Slack migration proof for the Channel Broker rollout. - Keeps native Slack default behavior unchanged; this only proves opt-in broker routing for Slack targets. - Stacks on Phase 2C Discord proof (#86156), Phase 2B Telegram proof (#86154), Phase 2A conformance baseline (#86153), and Phase 1 broker foundation (#86096). Closes #86112 Related #86108 Related #86105 Supersedes closed PR #86142, which was closed by the repository active-PR cap rather than by an implementation failure. ## What changed - Normalize broker-prefixed Slack DM targets such as `broker:slack:user:U12345678` into broker provider requests with `conversation.type = "direct"`. - Normalize broker-prefixed Slack channel thread targets such as `broker:slack:channel:C12345678?threadId=1716500000.000001` into broker provider requests with `conversation.type = "channel"` and `threadId`. - Canonicalize Slack broker session routes to the resolved platform target, preserving direct-route semantics as `slack:direct%3A` and thread routes as `slack:?threadId=`. ## Real Behavior Proof - **Behavior or issue addressed:** Slack DM and threaded-channel targets can route through `channel-broker` while preserving direct/channel conversation semantics and Slack `threadTs` as broker `threadId`. - **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout at `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`, using the Channel Broker plugin and SDK from the stacked rollout branch on May 24, 2026. - **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran a `node --import tsx --input-type=module` terminal proof that called `channelBrokerPlugin.message.send.text` with `to: "broker:slack:channel:C12345678?threadId=1716500000.000001"` and captured the provider request passed to the configured broker runtime. - **Evidence after fix:** Terminal output excerpt from that local command: ```json { "label": "slack-thread", "messageId": "native-slack-thread", "platform": "slack", "conversation": { "id": "C12345678", "type": "channel", "threadId": "1716500000.000001" }, "requirements": { "text": true, "thread": true } } ``` - **Observed result after fix:** The live plugin path produced the expected Slack provider request and kept threaded delivery represented by the broker `threadId` field. - **What was not tested:** Live Slack API traffic and the full local suite; this PR stays scoped to opt-in broker routing proof. Focused diff for this stack layer: https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-discord-routing...feat/channel-broker-slack-routing ## Validation Run locally from `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`: - `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/conformance.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-channel-broker.config.ts` - `pnpm exec tsc --noEmit -p extensions/channel-broker/tsconfig.json` - `git diff --check` - `pnpm exec oxfmt --check extensions/channel-broker/src/target.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts` - `pnpm exec oxlint extensions/channel-broker --format unix` ## Review state - Live for maintainer review; no longer draft. - No native Slack migration is enabled by default in this PR. - Includes a tiny test-isolation hardening for `src/agents/model-catalog-visibility.test.ts` after the unrelated `checks-node-agentic-agents` shard repeatedly timed out there while the single file passed locally.

OpenClaw keeps the shared semantics that repeatedly regress across native channel plugins: sessions, allowlists, routing, /verbose, streaming policy, durable final sends, receipts, retries, and auditability. The direction is intentionally incremental: first land the versioned broker SDK/plugin foundation, then prove conformance, then prove opt-in routing for the major channels, then model long-tail and constrained providers. Only after migration paths exist should legacy native-channel feature work freeze in #86115. ## Why This Is Better Today, one fix to streaming, /verbose, or delivery behavior can make Telegram fast this week and then break Discord, Slack, or another native plugin the next week. GitHub still shows `main` as the base for each PR so maintainers can land them one by one from fork branches; the branches themselves are stacked, and maintainers can review the focused adjacent diffs here: | PR | Focused adjacent diff | | --- | --- | | #86153 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-protocol...feat/channel-broker-conformance | | #86154 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-conformance...feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing | | #86156 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing...feat/channel-broker-discord-routing | | #86157 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-discord-routing...feat/channel-broker-slack-routing | | #86164 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-slack-routing...feat/channel-broker-official-platform-capabilities | | #86165 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-official-platform-capabilities...feat/channel-broker-constrained-platform-capabilities | ## This PR - Phase: Phase 2C - Discord opt-in broker routing proof - Role: Proves Discord DMs/channels/threads preserve conversation semantics through broker routing. - Head: `442d87e82b` ## Current Review State This PR is live for maintainer review. Current head: `442d87e82b`. Focused local validation for this phase is documented below. Broad validation remains owned by GitHub Actions. Native channel defaults remain unchanged unless this phase explicitly says otherwise. ## Summary - Adds the Phase 2C Discord broker routing proof on top of #86154, #86153, and #86096. - Normalizes broker-routed Discord DMs and channel threads without importing native Discord extension internals. - Preserves non-channel conversation type in canonical broker routes so Discord DMs remain direct routes. ## Rollout Context Closes #86111 Related #86108 Related #86105 Stacked on #86154 Stacked on #86153 Stacked on #86096 Supersedes closed PR #86139, which was closed by the repository active-PR cap rather than by an implementation failure. Native Discord defaults stay unchanged. Existing prefix ownership still keeps `discord:` owned by the native Discord plugin when installed; this PR proves the opt-in broker route through `broker:discord:...` and the broker alias path when native Discord is absent. For review, the focused stack diff is: https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing...feat/channel-broker-discord-routing ## Real Behavior Proof - **Behavior or issue addressed:** Discord DM and threaded-channel targets can route through `channel-broker` while preserving direct/channel conversation semantics and provider-owned ids. - **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout at `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`, using the Channel Broker plugin and SDK from the stacked rollout branch on May 24, 2026. - **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran a `node --import tsx --input-type=module` terminal proof that called `channelBrokerPlugin.message.send.text` with `to: "broker:discord:user:123456789012345678"` and captured the provider request passed to the configured broker runtime. - **Evidence after fix:** Terminal output excerpt from that local command: ```json { "label": "discord-dm", "messageId": "native-discord-dm", "platform": "discord", "conversation": { "id": "123456789012345678", "type": "direct" }, "requirements": { "text": true } } ``` - **Observed result after fix:** The live plugin path produced a direct Discord broker provider request rather than an ambiguous channel-looking target. - **What was not tested:** Live Discord API traffic and the full local suite; this PR stays scoped to opt-in broker routing proof. ### Behavior OpenClaw can represent Discord DMs and channel threads through `channel-broker` using stable broker semantics: provider platform `discord`, direct/channel conversation type, conversation id, and optional thread id. ### Environment - Local checkout: `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker` - Branch: `feat/channel-broker-discord-routing` - Head: `442d87e82b` (current Discord proof head) ### Steps 1. Sent a synthetic message to `broker:discord:user:123456789012345678`. 2. Verified the provider request used `platform: "discord"`, `conversation.type: "direct"`, and `conversation.id: "123456789012345678"`. 3. Sent a synthetic message to `broker:discord:channel:222222222222222222?threadId=333333333333333333`. 4. Verified the provider request used channel conversation id plus `threadId`. 5. Resolved the outbound session route for the Discord DM target. 6. Verified the route stayed direct and canonicalized to `discord:direct%3A123456789012345678`. ### Evidence - `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/conformance.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-channel-broker.config.ts` - 2 files passed, 13 tests passed. - `pnpm exec tsc --noEmit -p extensions/channel-broker/tsconfig.json` - passed. - `pnpm exec oxfmt --check extensions/channel-broker/src/target.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts` - passed. - `pnpm exec oxlint extensions/channel-broker --format unix` - passed. - `git diff --check` - passed. ### Observed Result Discord DMs and threads now flow through broker target parsing and provider request semantics, not through core or native Discord extension branches. ### Not Tested - Live Discord API traffic; this PR is the opt-in broker migration proof, not a live provider connector. - Full local suite. Per `/Volumes/LEXAR` policy, broad validation is left to GitHub Actions. ## Current Stack Gate Context This branch is rebased on the current Phase 1 foundation head. Focused local validation is documented above or below; broad validation remains on GitHub Actions.

For review, the focused stack diff is: https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-conformance...feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing ## Real Behavior Proof - **Behavior or issue addressed:** Telegram topic targets can route through `channel-broker` and become provider requests with canonical `telegram` platform, channel conversation id, and topic `threadId`. - **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout at `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`, using the Channel Broker plugin and SDK from the stacked rollout branch on May 24, 2026. - **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran a `node --import tsx --input-type=module` terminal proof that called `channelBrokerPlugin.message.send.text` with `to: "broker:telegram:-1001234567890:topic:42"` and captured the provider request passed to the configured broker runtime. - **Evidence after fix:** Terminal output excerpt from that local command: ```json { "label": "telegram-topic", "messageId": "native-telegram-topic", "platform": "telegram", "conversation": { "id": "-1001234567890", "type": "channel", "threadId": "42" }, "requirements": { "text": true, "thread": true } } ``` - **Observed result after fix:** The live plugin path normalized Telegram topic shorthand into the broker provider request shape without importing native Telegram extension internals. - **What was not tested:** Live Telegram Bot API traffic and the full local suite; this PR stays scoped to opt-in broker routing proof. ### Behavior OpenClaw can route a Telegram topic through `channel-broker` without Telegram extension code. The broker receives a normalized platform `telegram`, conversation id, and thread id; OpenClaw keeps the session route canonical and leaves native Telegram prefix ownership intact. ### Environment - Local checkout: `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker` - Branch: `feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing` - Head: `dbaca37c67` (current Telegram proof head) ### Steps 1. Sent a synthetic message to `broker:telegram:-1001234567890:topic:42` through `channelBrokerPlugin.message.send.text`. 2. Verified the provider request used `platform: "telegram"`, `conversation.id: "-1001234567890"`, and `conversation.threadId: "42"`. 3. Resolved the outbound session route for the same target. 4. Verified the route canonicalized to `telegram:-1001234567890?threadId=42` and preserved channel chat semantics. ### Evidence - `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/conformance.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-channel-broker.config.ts` - 2 files passed, 10 tests passed. - `pnpm exec tsc --noEmit -p extensions/channel-broker/tsconfig.json` - passed. - `pnpm exec oxfmt --check extensions/channel-broker/src/target.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts` - passed. - `pnpm exec oxlint extensions/channel-broker --format unix` - passed. - `git diff --check` - passed. ### Observed Result Telegram topic routing is represented by broker-owned target normalization and provider request semantics, not by core importing or calling native Telegram extension internals. ### Not Tested - Live Telegram Bot API traffic; this PR is the opt-in broker migration proof, not a live provider connector. - Full local suite. Per `/Volumes/LEXAR` policy, broad validation is left to GitHub Actions. ## Current Stack Gate Context This branch is rebased on the current Phase 1 foundation head. Focused local validation is documented above or below; broad validation remains on GitHub Actions.

target.title: [Feature]: Channel Broker Phase 2A conformance harness
target.body:
## Maintainer TL;DR This stack consolidates the recurring Telegram/Discord/Slack/WhatsApp/Signal/iMessage/etc. maintenance churn behind one Channel Broker contract. OpenClaw keeps the shared semantics that repeatedly regress across native channel plugins: sessions, allowlists, routing, /verbose, streaming policy, durable final sends, receipts, retries, and auditability. Broker providers own platform mechanics: bot APIs, bridge/device state, native ids, delivery quirks, and provider capabilities. This PR is the regression shield. It turns the recurring channel pain into shared conformance: signed inbound webhooks, normalization, dedupe, durable final sends, receipts, retryable failures, preview/progress gates, and /verbose channelData. The direction is intentionally incremental: first land the versioned broker SDK/plugin foundation, then prove conformance, then prove opt-in routing for the major channels, then model long-tail and constrained providers. Only after migration paths exist should legacy native-channel feature work freeze in #86115. ## Why This Is Better Today, one fix to streaming, /verbose, or delivery behavior can make Telegram fast this week and then break Discord, Slack, or another native plugin the next week. The broker path makes those shared semantics one OpenClaw-owned contract instead of many platform-specific re-implementations. When a provider adds support for previews, threads, receipts, media, or constrained account modes, that capability can become available through the same contract to routed channels whose provider declares and passes conformance for it. ## Stack Shape ```mermaid flowchart TD U["Umbrella issue #86105: reduce maintained-channel churn"] --> P1["#86096 Phase 1: SDK + bundled broker foundation"] P1 --> P2A["#86153 Phase 2A: conformance + signed inbound"] P2A --> P2B["#86154 Phase 2B: Telegram opt-in proof"] P2B --> P2C["#86156 Phase 2C: Discord opt-in proof"] P2C --> P2D["#86157 Phase 2D: Slack opt-in proof"] P2D --> P3["#86164 Phase 3: official/app capability matrix"] P3 --> P4["#86165 Phase 4: constrained provider capabilities"] P4 -. "future, not in this stack" .-> P5["Future freeze issue #86115: legacy native-plugin freeze"] ``` | PR | Role in the consolidation | | --- | --- | | #86096 | Creates the versioned broker SDK/plugin foundation, provider routing guard, media final-send mapping, SecretRef/cancellation handling, and prefix-ownership contract. | | #86153 | Proves shared lifecycle conformance and adds signed inbound webhook registration/runtime handoff: inbound dedupe, durable final sends, receipts, retryable failures, preview/progress gates, and /verbose channelData. | | #86154 | Proves Telegram topics can route through broker targets without native Telegram internals. | | #86156 | Proves Discord DMs/channels/threads preserve conversation semantics through broker routing. | | #86157 | Proves Slack DMs/channel threads preserve direct/thread semantics through broker routing. | | #86164 | Adds official/app platform aliases, capability matrix rows, and declared delivery capability enforcement for long-tail channels. | | #86165 | Adds constrained-provider metadata for WhatsApp, Signal, iMessage, and bridge-backed providers. | ## Ownership Model ```mermaid flowchart LR Core["OpenClaw core
sessions, routing, allowlists
/verbose, streaming, retries
receipts, audit"] --> Broker["channel-broker plugin
openclaw/plugin-sdk/channel-broker
V1 protocol"] Broker --> Provider["Provider-owned broker
HTTP/webhook transport
platform adapters"] Provider --> Platforms["Telegram, Discord, Slack
Teams, Matrix, WhatsApp
Signal, iMessage, long tail"] Provider --> Broker Broker --> Core ``` ## Review Shape These PRs are intentionally linear. GitHub still shows `main` as the base for each PR so maintainers can land them one by one from fork branches; the branches themselves are stacked, and maintainers can review the focused adjacent diffs here: | PR | Focused adjacent diff | | --- | --- | | #86153 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-protocol...feat/channel-broker-conformance | | #86154 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-conformance...feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing | | #86156 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing...feat/channel-broker-discord-routing | | #86157 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-discord-routing...feat/channel-broker-slack-routing | | #86164 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-slack-routing...feat/channel-broker-official-platform-capabilities | | #86165 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-official-platform-capabilities...feat/channel-broker-constrained-platform-capabilities | ## This PR - Phase: Phase 2A - Conformance and signed inbound baseline - Role: Proves shared lifecycle conformance and adds signed inbound webhook registration/runtime handoff. - Head: `d218c81d5c` ## Current Review State This PR is live for maintainer review. Current head: `d218c81d5c`. Focused local validation for this phase is documented below. Broad validation remains owned by GitHub Actions. Native channel defaults remain unchanged unless this phase explicitly says otherwise. ## Summary - Adds the Phase 2A Channel Broker conformance baseline on top of #86096. - Extends `openclaw/plugin-sdk/channel-broker` with inbound normalization, dedupe-key, provider capability normalization, and platform capability checks. - Adds bundled broker conformance coverage for durable inbound receive, durable final send receipts, retryable receipt failures, preview/progress capability gates, and `/verbose`/tool-message `channelData`. ## Rollout Context Closes #86109 Related #86108 Related #86105 Stacked on #86096 Supersedes closed PR #86133, which was closed by the repository active-PR cap rather than by an implementation failure. This PR intentionally does not migrate Telegram, Discord, or Slack routing. Native defaults stay unchanged until the Phase 2B/2C/2D proofs land behind opt-in broker routing. For review, the focused stack diff is: https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-protocol...feat/channel-broker-conformance ## Real Behavior Proof - **Behavior or issue addressed:** The broker conformance baseline normalizes inbound events, builds durable dedupe keys, evaluates preview/progress capabilities, and sends outbound requests through the bundled broker lifecycle without platform-specific core branches. - **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout at `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`, using the Channel Broker plugin and SDK from the stacked rollout branch on May 24, 2026. - **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran a `node --import tsx --input-type=module` terminal proof that imported `src/plugin-sdk/channel-broker.ts`, created a Telegram inbound event, built the durable dedupe key, evaluated Discord preview/progress capabilities, and sent broker outbound requests through `channelBrokerPlugin.message.send.text`. - **Evidence after fix:** Terminal output excerpt from that local command: ```json { "inboundDedupeKey": "acme:bot-main:telegram:live-proof-telegram-1", "previewProgressSupported": true } ``` - **Observed result after fix:** The live SDK/plugin path produced the expected durable inbound key and capability decision outside the Vitest harness, proving the Phase 2A contract surface can carry the normalized lifecycle facts that later channel migrations consume. - **What was not tested:** Live provider network traffic and the full local suite; those remain covered by later provider proofs and GitHub Actions. ### Behavior The broker contract now has a conformance harness for the lifecycle paths that regress most often across maintained chat channels: inbound normalization/dedupe, durable final sends, provider receipts, retry/failure propagation, preview/progress capability checks, and `/verbose` tool-message metadata. ### Environment - Local checkout: `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker` - Branch: `feat/channel-broker-conformance` - Head: `d218c81d5c` (current Phase 2A head) ### Steps 1. Normalized a synthetic Telegram inbound event through the SDK and accepted it through the durable inbound receive journal. 2. Replayed the same dedupe key while pending and after completion to prove duplicate/redelivery handling. 3. Sent a synthetic Slack final message through the bundled broker runtime and verified provider receipt commit material. 4. Returned a retryable Discord provider receipt and verified the send fails with structured receipt context. 5. Built preview/finalize broker requests and asserted Discord preview/progress support through declared capabilities. 6. Passed `/verbose` tool-message policy through broker `channelData` without platform-specific branching. ### Evidence - `OPENCLAW_VITEST_INCLUDE_FILE=/Volumes/LEXAR/Codex/channel-broker-vitest-include.json pnpm exec vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts` - 1 file passed, 7 tests passed. - `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/conformance.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/secret-contract.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-channel-broker.config.ts` - 3 files passed, 10 tests passed. - `pnpm exec tsc --noEmit -p extensions/channel-broker/tsconfig.json` - passed. - `pnpm tsgo:core:test` - passed. - `pnpm exec oxfmt --check src/plugin-sdk/channel-broker.ts src/plugin-sdk/channel-broker.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/outbound.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/conformance.test.ts` - passed. - `pnpm exec oxlint extensions/channel-broker --format unix` - passed. - `git diff --check` - passed. ### Observed Result The SDK helpers normalize provider payloads without dropping native metadata, the bundled plugin refuses non-sent provider receipts as durable-send failures, and conformance tests prove the current broker path can represent the Phase 2 lifecycle requirements without changing user-visible channel defaults. ### Not Tested - Full local suite. Per `/Volumes/LEXAR` policy, broad validation is left to GitHub Actions. - Live Telegram, Discord, or Slack provider traffic. Those are intentionally scoped to Phase 2B/2C/2D. ## Current Stack Gate Context This branch is rebased on the current Phase 1 foundation head. Focused local validation is documented above or below; broad validation remains on GitHub Actions.

target.title: [Feature]: Channel Broker Phase 1 protocol foundation
target.body:
## Maintainer TL;DR This stack consolidates the recurring Telegram/Discord/Slack/WhatsApp/Signal/iMessage/etc. maintenance churn behind one Channel Broker contract. OpenClaw keeps the shared semantics that repeatedly regress across native channel plugins: sessions, allowlists, routing, /verbose, streaming policy, durable final sends, receipts, retries, and auditability. Broker providers own platform mechanics: bot APIs, bridge/device state, native ids, delivery quirks, and provider capabilities. This PR is the narrow foundation. It creates the versioned broker SDK subpath and bundled channel-broker plugin, but it does not move Telegram, Discord, Slack, or any user defaults. The direction is intentionally incremental: first land the versioned broker SDK/plugin foundation, then prove conformance, then prove opt-in routing for the major channels, then model long-tail and constrained providers. Only after migration paths exist should legacy native-channel feature work freeze in #86115. ## Why This Is Better Today, one fix to streaming, /verbose, or delivery behavior can make Telegram fast this week and then break Discord, Slack, or another native plugin the next week. The broker path makes those shared semantics one OpenClaw-owned contract instead of many platform-specific re-implementations. When a provider adds support for previews, threads, receipts, media, or constrained account modes, that capability can become available through the same contract to routed channels whose provider declares and passes conformance for it. ## Stack Shape ```mermaid flowchart TD U["Umbrella issue #86105: reduce maintained-channel churn"] --> P1["#86096 Phase 1: SDK + bundled broker foundation"] P1 --> P2A["#86153 Phase 2A: conformance + signed inbound"] P2A --> P2B["#86154 Phase 2B: Telegram opt-in proof"] P2B --> P2C["#86156 Phase 2C: Discord opt-in proof"] P2C --> P2D["#86157 Phase 2D: Slack opt-in proof"] P2D --> P3["#86164 Phase 3: official/app capability matrix"] P3 --> P4["#86165 Phase 4: constrained provider capabilities"] P4 -. "future, not in this stack" .-> P5["Future freeze issue #86115: legacy native-plugin freeze"] ``` | PR | Role in the consolidation | | --- | --- | | #86096 | Creates the versioned broker SDK/plugin foundation, provider routing guard, media final-send mapping, SecretRef/cancellation handling, and prefix-ownership contract. | | #86153 | Proves shared lifecycle conformance and adds signed inbound webhook registration/runtime handoff: inbound dedupe, durable final sends, receipts, retryable failures, preview/progress gates, and /verbose channelData. | | #86154 | Proves Telegram topics can route through broker targets without native Telegram internals. | | #86156 | Proves Discord DMs/channels/threads preserve conversation semantics through broker routing. | | #86157 | Proves Slack DMs/channel threads preserve direct/thread semantics through broker routing. | | #86164 | Adds official/app platform aliases, capability matrix rows, and declared delivery capability enforcement for long-tail channels. | | #86165 | Adds constrained-provider metadata for WhatsApp, Signal, iMessage, and bridge-backed providers. | ## Ownership Model ```mermaid flowchart LR Core["OpenClaw core
sessions, routing, allowlists
/verbose, streaming, retries
receipts, audit"] --> Broker["channel-broker plugin
openclaw/plugin-sdk/channel-broker
V1 protocol"] Broker --> Provider["Provider-owned broker
HTTP/webhook transport
platform adapters"] Provider --> Platforms["Telegram, Discord, Slack
Teams, Matrix, WhatsApp
Signal, iMessage, long tail"] Provider --> Broker Broker --> Core ``` ## Review Shape These PRs are intentionally linear. GitHub still shows `main` as the base for each PR so maintainers can land them one by one from fork branches; the branches themselves are stacked, and maintainers can review the focused adjacent diffs here: | PR | Focused adjacent diff | | --- | --- | | #86153 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-protocol...feat/channel-broker-conformance | | #86154 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-conformance...feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing | | #86156 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-telegram-routing...feat/channel-broker-discord-routing | | #86157 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-discord-routing...feat/channel-broker-slack-routing | | #86164 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-slack-routing...feat/channel-broker-official-platform-capabilities | | #86165 | https://github.com/100yenadmin/openclaw/compare/feat/channel-broker-official-platform-capabilities...feat/channel-broker-constrained-platform-capabilities | ## This PR - Phase: Phase 1 - SDK and bundled broker foundation - Role: Creates the versioned broker SDK/plugin foundation, provider routing guard, media final-send mapping, SecretRef/cancellation handling, and prefix-ownership contract. - Head: `9e55f11ef9` ## Current Review State This PR is live for maintainer review. Current head: `9e55f11ef9`. Focused local validation for this phase is documented below. Broad validation remains owned by GitHub Actions. Native channel defaults remain unchanged unless this phase explicitly says otherwise. ## Summary Adds Phase 1 of the Channel Broker consolidation program: a versioned `openclaw/plugin-sdk/channel-broker` protocol surface, a bundled `channel-broker` channel plugin, provider-owned outbound request/receipt helpers, docs, and focused tests. This PR intentionally stays at the protocol/foundation layer. Telegram, Discord, Slack, and later channel migrations are tracked separately and should not be folded into this PR. ## Linked Context Closes #86107 Related #86105 Related #86108 Related #86109 Related #86110 Related #86111 Related #86112 Related #86113 Related #86114 Related #86115 ## Review Repair History The current Phase 1 branch includes focused repairs for ClawSweeper's blocker classes: - Preserves native prefix ownership when `channel-broker` declares platform aliases that overlap native plugins. If Telegram/Discord/Slack are registered, their prefixes stay native-owned; broker aliases own a platform prefix only when no native plugin declares it. - Adds the standard channel SecretRef contract for `channels.channel-broker.{outboundToken,signingSecret}` plus `accounts.*` and `providers.*` variants, and changes the broker schema/docs to use SecretRef-compatible credential input. - Threads `AbortSignal` through broker outbound requests, including the HTTP `fetch` transport and custom runtime override path. ## Real Behavior Proof - **Behavior or issue addressed:** Verified the Phase 1 broker surface resolves as a bundled channel and produces a normalized V1 outbound request for a Telegram-scoped broker target. This proves the user-visible foundation being shipped in this PR: catalog discovery, channel id normalization, target construction, protocol versioning, platform normalization, thread preservation, and final-send payload shape. - **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout at `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`, branch `feat/channel-broker-protocol`, after the current Phase 1 repair/doc series through `d39d47b3db`. - **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** ```bash node --import tsx -e 'const { listBundledChannelCatalogEntries } = await import("./src/channels/bundled-channel-catalog-read.ts"); const { normalizeChatChannelId } = await import("./src/channels/ids.ts"); const { buildBrokerConversationTarget, createBrokerOutboundRequest } = await import("./src/plugin-sdk/channel-broker.ts"); const catalog = listBundledChannelCatalogEntries().find((entry) => entry.id === "channel-broker"); const target = buildBrokerConversationTarget({ platform: "Telegram", conversationId: "chat 123", threadId: "topic-7" }); const request = createBrokerOutboundRequest({ requestId: "send-proof-1", providerId: "synthetic-provider", platform: "Telegram", accountId: "default", conversation: { id: "chat 123", type: "channel", threadId: "topic-7" }, mode: "final", payloads: [{ text: "broker proof" }], requirements: { text: true, thread: true } }); console.log(JSON.stringify({ discoveredChannel: catalog && { id: catalog.id, order: catalog.order, docsPath: catalog.channel.docsPath }, channelNormalization: normalizeChatChannelId("channel-broker"), target, outboundProtocolVersion: request.version, outboundMode: request.mode, outboundPlatform: request.platform, outboundConversation: request.conversation, outboundPayloadText: request.payloads[0]?.text }, null, 2));' ``` - **Evidence after fix:** Terminal output copied from the command above: ```json { "discoveredChannel": { "id": "channel-broker", "order": 60, "docsPath": "/channels/channel-broker" }, "channelNormalization": "channel-broker", "target": "telegram:chat%20123?threadId=topic-7", "outboundProtocolVersion": 1, "outboundMode": "final", "outboundPlatform": "telegram", "outboundConversation": { "id": "chat 123", "type": "channel", "threadId": "topic-7" }, "outboundPayloadText": "broker proof" } ``` - **Observed result after fix:** OpenClaw discovered the bundled `channel-broker` entry, normalized the channel id, built a Telegram broker target that preserved `threadId`, and emitted a V1 final outbound request with normalized `platform: "telegram"`, `conversation.threadId: "topic-7"`, and payload text `broker proof`. - **What was not tested:** No real third-party broker provider delivery was tested in Phase 1. That is intentionally deferred to the Phase 2 migration proof issues for Telegram, Discord, and Slack. ## Focused Local Validation All local validation below ran from `/Volumes/LEXAR/repos/openclaw-channel-broker`: ```bash git diff --check ``` ```bash node node_modules/oxfmt/bin/oxfmt --check --threads=1 --config .oxfmtrc.jsonc docs/channels/channel-broker.md docs/reference/secretref-credential-surface.md extensions/channel-broker/src/accounts.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/config-schema.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/outbound.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/runtime.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/secret-contract.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/secret-contract.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/types.ts src/infra/outbound/channel-target-prefix.ts src/infra/outbound/channel-target-prefix.test.ts ``` ```bash pnpm exec vitest run extensions/channel-broker/src/channel.test.ts extensions/channel-broker/src/secret-contract.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-channel-broker.config.ts ``` ```bash pnpm exec vitest run src/infra/outbound/channel-target-prefix.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.config.ts ``` ```bash pnpm exec tsc --noEmit -p extensions/channel-broker/tsconfig.json ``` ```bash pnpm exec vitest run test/scripts/bundled-plugin-build-entries.test.ts --config test/vitest/vitest.config.ts ``` ## Known Inherited Gates - `pnpm plugin-sdk:surface:check` has an inherited clean-upstream SDK budget drift; this PR adds the intended `plugin-sdk/channel-broker` public subpath. - `pnpm docs:check-i18n-glossary` has inherited unrelated glossary-label failures on clean upstream; Channel Broker glossary entries were added in this branch.

target.title: Policy: add ingress channel conformance checks
target.body:
## Summary

Adds config-level Policy conformance checks for ingress/channel access posture:

```jsonc
{
  "ingress": {
    "session": {
      "requireDmScope": "per-channel-peer"
    },
    "channels": {
      "allowDmPolicies": ["pairing", "allowlist", "disabled"],
      "denyOpenGroups": true,
      "requireMentionInGroups": true
    }
  }
}
```

This stays in the config-conformance lane: it observes authored OpenClaw config, emits policy/doctor findings for drift, and does not add runtime enforcement.

The PR also lets `scopes.agents.<scopeName>.ingress` use the scoped-agent container from #85817. Current ingress evidence is default workspace/channel posture, so scoped ingress is intentionally limited to `agentIds` that normalize to `main` until non-default agent ingress evidence exists.

## Verification

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/policy/src/doctor/register.test.ts extensions/policy/src/cli.test.ts -- --reporter=dot` -> 2 files, 232 tests passed
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check docs/cli/policy.md docs/plugins/reference/policy.md docs/plan/policy-agent-scoped-overlays.md extensions/policy/src/doctor/register.ts extensions/policy/src/doctor/register.test.ts extensions/policy/src/policy-state.ts`
- `pnpm exec oxlint extensions/policy/src/doctor/register.ts extensions/policy/src/doctor/register.test.ts extensions/policy/src/policy-state.ts`
- `pnpm docs:check-mdx docs/cli/policy.md docs/plugins/reference/policy.md docs/plan/policy-agent-scoped-overlays.md`
- `git diff --check refs/remotes/fork/policy-agent-scoped-design...HEAD`

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Policy can now express direct-message scope, channel DM policy, open-group denial, group mention-gate, and default scoped-ingress conformance requirements.

Real environment tested: WSL Ubuntu 24.04 checkout at `/root/src/openclaw-85744`, rebased on the updated #85817 stack, using disposable `OPENCLAW_HOME`, disposable `OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH`, and disposable `policy.jsonc` files under `/tmp`.

Exact command run after this patch for each proof case:

```bash
OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH="$proof/<case>.openclaw.json" \
  OPENCLAW_HOME="$proof/<case>-home" \
  OPENCLAW_DISABLE_AUTO_UPDATE=1 \
  node --import tsx src/entry.ts plugins enable policy

OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH="$proof/<case>.openclaw.json" \
  OPENCLAW_HOME="$proof/<case>-home" \
  OPENCLAW_DISABLE_AUTO_UPDATE=1 \
  node --import tsx src/entry.ts policy check --json
```

Failing ingress config used `session.dmScope=main`, Telegram `dmPolicy=open`, `groupPolicy=open`, and `groups.ops.requireMention=false` against policy requiring `per-channel-peer`, DM policies `pairing|allowlist|disabled`, no open groups, and mention gates in groups.

Failing output summary:

```json
{
  "ok": false,
  "findings": [
    {
      "checkId": "policy/ingress-dm-scope-unapproved",
      "ocPath": "oc://openclaw.config/session/dmScope",
      "requirement": "oc://failing.policy.jsonc/ingress/session/requireDmScope"
    },
    {
      "checkId": "policy/ingress-dm-policy-unapproved",
      "ocPath": "oc://openclaw.config/channels/telegram/dmPolicy",
      "requirement": "oc://failing.policy.jsonc/ingress/channels/allowDmPolicies"
    },
    {
      "checkId": "policy/ingress-open-groups-denied",
      "ocPath": "oc://openclaw.config/channels/telegram/groupPolicy",
      "requirement": "oc://failing.policy.jsonc/ingress/channels/denyOpenGroups"
    },
    {
      "checkId": "policy/ingress-group-mention-required",

    }

}
```

```json

}
```

target.title: Policy: add ingress channel conformance checks
target.body:
## Summary

Adds config-level Policy conformance checks for ingress/channel access posture:

```jsonc
{
  "ingress": {
    "session": {
      "requireDmScope": "per-channel-peer"
    },
    "channels": {
      "allowDmPolicies": ["pairing", "allowlist", "disabled"],
      "denyOpenGroups": true,
      "requireMentionInGroups": true
    }
  }
}
```

This stays in the config-conformance lane: it observes authored OpenClaw config, emits policy/doctor findings for drift, and does not add runtime enforcement.

The PR also lets `scopes.agents.<scopeName>.ingress` use the scoped-agent container from #85817. Current ingress evidence is default workspace/channel posture, so scoped ingress is intentionally limited to `agentIds` that normalize to `main` until non-default agent ingress evidence exists.

## Real behavior proof

target.title: fix(auto-reply): strip reasoning blocks before NO_REPLY detection (#66701)
target.body:
## Summary

`isSilentReplyText` in `src/auto-reply/tokens.ts` uses an exact-match regex (`^\s*NO_REPLY\s*$`) to suppress agent replies. Some models can prepend reasoning blocks before the token:

```text
<think>
Cav is talking to someone else. I will stay quiet here.
</think>NO_REPLY
```

The regex fails on this input and the raw reasoning text is posted to chat.

This patch calls `stripReasoningTagsFromText` from `src/shared/text/reasoning-tags.ts` before testing the input. That strips supported reasoning tags such as `<think>`, `<thinking>`, and `<antthinking>` before the exact `NO_REPLY` match.

The #19537 behavior is covered by tests: substantive replies that end with `NO_REPLY` are still not suppressed after reasoning-tag stripping.

Closes #66701.

## Changes

- `src/auto-reply/tokens.ts` — import `stripReasoningTagsFromText` and strip reasoning tags before the exact-match check in `isSilentReplyText`
- `src/auto-reply/tokens.test.ts` — 7 new test cases for reasoning-prefix variants, multiple blocks, token-inside-block, and unclosed-block cases

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: `isSilentReplyText("<think>I will stay quiet.</think>NO_REPLY")` returned `false`, which allowed reasoning text to be posted to chat; it now returns `true`.

Real environment tested: unit test suite `src/auto-reply/tokens.test.ts`

Exact steps or command run after this patch: `pnpm test src/auto-reply/tokens.test.ts --reporter=verbose`

Evidence after fix:
```text
✓ isSilentReplyText > returns true when <think> block precedes token (#66701)
✓ isSilentReplyText > returns true when <think> block with whitespace precedes token (#66701)
✓ isSilentReplyText > returns true for <antthinking> block prefix (#66701)
✓ isSilentReplyText > returns false when substantive text follows token after reasoning strip (#66701)
✓ isSilentReplyText > returns true for multiple sequential reasoning blocks before token (#66701)
✓ isSilentReplyText > returns false when token is inside a reasoning block with content after (#66701)
✓ isSilentReplyText > returns false for unclosed reasoning block containing substantive text before token (#66701)
Tests: 31 passed (31)
```

Observed result after fix: all 31 tests pass; the 7 new #66701 cases cover the pure-function fix surface.

What was not tested: live Telegram, Signal, or Slack delivery with a real Gemini or Haiku model producing a reasoning-prefixed `NO_REPLY`. Crabbox proof would cover that full path.

target.body:
## Summary

- add a shared strict positive-integer Commander option parser
- use it for `plugins search --limit` and `skills search --limit`
- cover the helper and both CLI parse paths so invalid limits stop before ClawHub calls

Fixes #84473.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** `plugins search --limit` and `skills search --limit` no longer accept partially numeric values such as `1abc`; they fail during CLI option parsing before calling ClawHub.
- **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw source checkout on macOS, branch `fix/84473-search-limit-parser`, commit `b2c536f8cd55e0cd7f61b916cc0c23a243460a04`, Node v24.11.1.

- **Evidence after fix:** Copied terminal output from the local source checkout:

```console
$ node --import tsx src/entry.ts plugins search calendar --limit 1abc --json
OpenClaw could not parse this command: option '--limit <n>' argument '1abc' is invalid. Expected a positive integer.
Try: openclaw plugins search --help
```

```console
$ node --import tsx src/entry.ts skills search calendar --limit 1abc --json
OpenClaw could not parse this command: option '--limit <n>' argument '1abc' is invalid. Expected a positive integer.

```

Expected a positive integer.`, and `--limit -5` prints the same error for `-5`, all before any ClawHub call.

target.title: Fail Codex compaction at the Codex boundary
target.body:
## Summary

Codex runtime compaction was still being treated like an OpenClaw-owned PI safeguard in a few places. OpenClaw could preflight-compact Codex reply turns, post-turn compact Codex CLI transcripts, let a context-engine plugin replace Codex app-server compaction, and force plugin compaction before or after Codex `turn/start` overflow handling.

That is the wrong boundary. Codex already owns automatic compaction inside the Codex runtime. OpenClaw should not predict when a Codex thread needs compaction and then rewrite the transcript around it. At best that duplicates the native harness. At worst it crosses runtime/auth boundaries, restarts the shared app-server, or drifts a Codex-backed OpenAI session into OpenClaw/public Responses summarization.

This tightens the contract. Automatic compaction for Codex runtime sessions is native Codex-owned. OpenClaw now skips its preflight and CLI automatic compaction paths for Codex, removes the Codex app-server context-engine forced-compaction paths, and no longer lets an `ownsCompaction` context engine take over Codex app-server compaction. Explicit compaction is still supported: `/compact` and plugin-requested/manual compaction requests are forwarded to Codex as native `thread/compact/start`, then OpenClaw returns immediately instead of waiting, timing out, restarting the shared app-server, or falling back to another summarizer.

The fail-closed behavior stays where it belongs. If native Codex compaction cannot be started because the native thread binding is missing or stale, OpenClaw reports that failure honestly. It does not restart the shared Codex app-server as a retry strategy, and it does not fall back to OpenClaw/public OpenAI compaction for Codex runtime sessions. Non-Codex pinned harnesses still keep their previous context-engine fallback path.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Codex-runtime sessions should not be automatically compacted by OpenClaw preflight, CLI, context-engine, or public OpenAI summarization paths. Explicit/manual/plugin compaction should only start native Codex compaction through `thread/compact/start`, without OpenClaw waiting on completion, imposing its own timeout, restarting the shared app-server, or falling back to another compaction backend.

Real environment tested: Pash's local dev agent was restarted from this branch at commit `81328167c4be2e26adf2a0667ed19d3b5d99648a`; Gateway reported `2026.5.24` running from `/Users/pash/code/openclaw/dist/index.js`, pid `35893`, healthy loopback on `127.0.0.1:18789`. The live proof used the real `main` agent with Codex OAuth auth redacted, provider `openai-codex`, model `gpt-5.5`, and the embedded Codex app-server harness.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: `pnpm openclaw gateway restart`; `pnpm openclaw gateway status --deep`; `pnpm openclaw agent --agent main --session-key agent:main:codex-compaction-proof-85958-final-1779663763 --message "Final live proof turn 1 for PR 85958 on commit 81328167c4. Reply exactly: FINAL_PROOF_TURN_1_OK" --thinking minimal --timeout 600 --json`; `pnpm openclaw agent --agent main --session-key agent:main:codex-compaction-proof-85958-final-1779663763 --message "Final live proof turn 2 for PR 85958 on the same session. Reply exactly: FINAL_PROOF_TURN_2_OK" --thinking minimal --timeout 600 --json`; `rg -n 'preflight_compacting|compaction-diag|falling back to context engine|Summarization failed|provider_error_4xx|"api":"openai-responses"|"modelApi":"openai-responses"|thread/compact/start|contextCompaction|gateway_timeout|EMBEDDED FALLBACK' /Users/pash/.openclaw/agents/main/sessions/7b1d9666-d817-4182-b3a4-43ca985d530f*`; `rg -n 'context_compacted|contextCompaction|thread/compact|Summarization failed|api.responses.write|openai-responses|Incorrect API key|falling back to context engine' /Users/pash/.openclaw/agents/main/agent/codex-home/sessions/2026/05/24/rollout-2026-05-24T16-02-55-019e5c3a-2533-7321-845e-44a55ab1545b.jsonl`; `pnpm test extensions/codex/src/app-server/compact.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/commands-compact.test.ts src/commands/agent-via-gateway.test.ts -- --reporter=dot`; `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 extensions/codex/src/app-server/compact.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/compact.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/commands-compact.ts src/auto-reply/reply/commands-compact.test.ts src/commands/agent-via-gateway.ts src/commands/agent-via-gateway.test.ts`; `git diff --check`; `pnpm check:changed`; `pnpm build`.

Evidence after fix: The two live Gateway turns both completed in the same session, `7b1d9666-d817-4182-b3a4-43ca985d530f`, bound to Codex thread `019e5c3a-2533-7321-845e-44a55ab1545b`. Turn 1 returned exactly `FINAL_PROOF_TURN_1_OK` with run id `4a70de03-f779-488b-99d4-b2e4e362ea5b`, provider `openai-codex`, model `gpt-5.5`, `agentHarnessId: codex`, `fallbackUsed: false`, and runner `embedded`. Turn 2 returned exactly `FINAL_PROOF_TURN_2_OK` with run id `dfbb3821-57c6-44e7-972e-47e66a71f3ee`, the same session id, provider `openai-codex`, model `gpt-5.5`, `agentHarnessId: codex`, `fallbackUsed: false`, and runner `embedded`. The session-file grep found no OpenClaw preflight compaction, context-engine fallback, public `openai-responses` metadata, manual compact start, Gateway timeout fallback, or embedded fallback artifacts. The Codex rollout grep found no Codex compaction failure, public OpenAI Responses summarization, `api.responses.write` failure, or context-engine fallback strings.

Observed result after fix: The live dev agent stayed on the Codex runtime for normal turns and did not drift into OpenClaw/public Responses compaction. The code-level tests now lock the policy down: Codex app-server explicit/manual compaction starts `thread/compact/start` and does not wait for notification completion, add OpenClaw timeouts, restart the app-server, call context-engine compaction, or run context-engine maintenance as if compaction had completed; `/compact` reports `Codex compaction started` for this pending native path; and a Gateway timeout on `/compact` does not create a fresh embedded fallback session. Targeted tests, changed checks, formatting, diff whitespace checks, and build all passed locally, and exact-head PR CI is green.

What was not tested: I did not force a destructive live compaction timeout on Pash's running dev agent session, and I did not trigger a live Discord or Telegram human-authored `/compact` command from this shell. The native compact-start path and no-fallback behavior are covered by focused tests around the same runtime seam.

target.title: fix(telegram): transient Telegram pairing prompts
target.body: ## Summary The behavior I'm seeing is transient Telegram pairing requests after an idle: BEFORE After a deep dive this is most likely caused by FD pressure after returning from idle - I've ONLY experienced on return from idle. In that case, I think it should error not send a confusing and potentially time-consuming pairing request. This replication was done with a chmod 800 to have same effect. AFTER fail loudly, better than spurious pairing request. I've added a comment in code to consider backoff retries to wait till FD pressure lowers, but feels a bit edgecasey and clever for now. ## Solution - Avoid treating transient Telegram pairing-store read failures as an empty pairing allowlist that triggers a new pairing challenge. - Skip pairing-store reads when the effective DM policy is not pairing, or when configured `allowFrom` already authorizes the sender. - Preserve per-DM pairing policy resolution when flushing long text fragments. ## Verification - `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/telegram/src/bot.create-telegram-bot.test.ts` -> 91 passed - `node scripts/test-projects.mjs extensions/telegram/src/bot.media.stickers-and-fragments.e2e.test.ts` -> 5 passed - `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 extensions/telegram/src/bot.media.stickers-and-fragments.e2e.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot/helpers.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot-handlers.runtime.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot-native-commands.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot.create-telegram-bot.test.ts` -> passed - `git diff --check` -> passed - `AUTOREVIEW_AUTO_TESTS=0 .agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode local` -> clean, no accepted/actionable findings Behavior addressed: Same private Telegram chat can receive a transient pairing-store read failure without getting a bogus pairing prompt, then respond normally once the next store read succeeds. Real environment tested: Local Codex worktree on Node/Vitest with mocked Telegram bot handlers and pairing-store dependency failures. Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran the two focused Telegram test files above, formatter check, `git diff --check`, and autoreview. Evidence after fix: `bot.create-telegram-bot.test.ts` passes 91 tests, including the same-chat back-to-back message regression where the first store read rejects and the second returns the paired sender id. Observed result after fix: No pairing request is upserted on transient store failure; configured/open DMs avoid unnecessary store reads; per-DM pairing store authorization still works for text-fragment flushes. What was not tested: No live Telegram round trip was run; this is covered with mocked bot handler regressions rather than Crabbox/live Telegram proof.

## Summary

## Real behavior proof

- Evidence after fix: Console output from the temp packaged-launcher smoke:

```text
$ tmp=$(mktemp -d) && cp openclaw.mjs "$tmp/openclaw.mjs" && printf '{"version":"test","type":"module"}\n' > "$tmp/package.json" && mkdir -p "$tmp/dist" && printf 'console.log("entry loaded")\n' > "$tmp/dist/entry.js" && (cd "$tmp" && bun ./openclaw.mjs)
entry loaded
```

target.title: fix(gateway): clear admin scopes for backend self-pairing
target.body:
Summary
- Stop treating shared token/password auth as sufficient to preserve any self-declared operator scopes for local backend self-pairing.
- Keep unscoped backend local handoff intact, while routing scoped local backend Gateway calls through normal device identity so internal loopback calls still work.
- Add explicit issue coverage for loopback/no-Origin backend identity + shared token/password + self-declared operator scopes + no device identity.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** Device-less local/shared-secret backend Gateway clients must not preserve self-declared operator scopes while skipping pairing.

**Real environment tested:** Temporary local OpenClaw Gateway from this PR branch, token auth, loopback WebSocket at `ws://127.0.0.1:39139`, channels skipped, configured via a temporary `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR` and `OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH`.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Started the local Gateway from this branch, then ran a Node/tsx WebSocket client using `GatewayClient` as `gateway-client` / `backend` with shared token auth, `deviceIdentity: null`, and `scopes: ["operator.write"]`. The client then attempted `sessions.list`.

**Evidence after fix:** Terminal output from the live local Gateway client:

```text
$ OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/tmp/openclaw-pr86192-proof-... \
  OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/tmp/openclaw-pr86192-proof-.../openclaw.json \
  OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_PORT=39139 \
  OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_TOKEN=secret \
  node --import tsx --input-type=module

local gateway: ws://127.0.0.1:39139
backend shared-token no-device requested scopes: [operator.write]
hello auth scopes: []
sessions.list error: missing scope: operator.read
```

Gateway runtime log excerpt for the same live connection:

```text
[ws] ⇄ res ✗ sessions.list 1ms errorCode=INVALID_REQUEST errorMessage=missing scope: operator.read
```

**Observed result after fix:** The backend shared-token, no-device connection completed, but the server returned no authorized scopes in `hello-ok`; the follow-up read-scoped RPC failed with `missing scope: operator.read`. This verifies that non-admin self-declared scopes are cleared too, not only `operator.admin`.

**What was not tested:** Remote browser-origin behavior was not live-tested here; focused regression coverage covers the local and shared-secret-loopback cases changed by this PR.

Additional verification
```text
$ pnpm exec vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway.config.ts src/gateway/call.test.ts src/gateway/server/ws-connection/handshake-auth-helpers.test.ts src/gateway/server.auth.compat-baseline.test.ts src/gateway/server.auth.control-ui.test.ts

Test Files  4 passed (4)
Tests  165 passed (165)

$ pnpm exec vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway-server.config.ts src/gateway/server.sessions-send.test.ts

Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests  3 passed (3)

$ pnpm exec vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents.config.ts src/agents/code-mode.test.ts

Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests  30 passed (30)

$ git diff --check
# no output
```

Fixes openclaw/openclaw#72418

target.title: Preserve TUI optimistic user messages across history reloads
target.body:
## Summary

- Preserve optimistic outbound TUI user messages as pending chat-log entries instead of plain rendered entries.
- Keep pending user messages across `chat.history` rebuilds, reconcile them once persisted history catches up, and clear only the failed pending send on `chat.send` errors.
- Add focused TUI regression coverage plus an Unreleased changelog entry.

## Related Issues

Refs #54722 and #59014 for the earlier closed TUI user-message disappearance regressions. Related but distinct from #78017, which tracks long-message scrollback loss in the underlying renderer.

## Real Behavior Proof

Behavior addressed: TUI user messages no longer disappear when a stale history reload or run-error refresh happens before the optimistic outbound message is present in persisted history.

Real environment tested: local OpenClaw source checkout on macOS, TUI unit config and fake-backend PTY harness.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```sh
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.tui.config.ts src/tui/components/chat-log.test.ts src/tui/tui-command-handlers.test.ts src/tui/tui-session-actions.test.ts src/tui/tui-event-handlers.test.ts --reporter=verbose
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.tui-pty.config.ts
git diff --check
.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode local
```

Evidence after fix: focused TUI tests passed 4 files / 115 tests; fake-backend PTY lane passed 1 file / 11 tests; `git diff --check` passed; autoreview reported no accepted/actionable findings.

Observed result after fix: optimistic user messages are kept through stale history rebuilds and committed/reconciled once the real run or persisted history catches up.

What was not tested: no live Gateway/provider run was executed; the fake-backend PTY harness does not prove Gateway transport, embedded runtime, providers, session persistence, or live streaming.

target.title: [AI-assisted] Add ClickClack slash command suggestions
target.body:
## Summary
- enrich the shared native command registry with usage/menu metadata for slash command autocomplete
- add a gateway-authenticated ClickClack plugin route at `POST /clickclack/commands/suggest` for composer slash command suggestions
- filter suggestions through ClickClack account allowlists plus `commands.allowFrom` and `commands.ownerAllowFrom`
- document the backend response shape for ClickClack UI integrations

## Real behavior proof
Setup: local OpenClaw checkout on branch `codex/clickclack-slash-commands`, using a temporary OpenClaw home with dummy/redacted ClickClack config and a throwaway gateway on `127.0.0.1:18799`. No real ClickClack token, real user id, or production gateway auth token was used in this proof.

Temporary proof config used these redacted values:

```json
{
  "gateway": { "mode": "local", "auth": { "mode": "token", "token": "proof-token-redacted" } },
  "commands": {
    "allowFrom": { "clickclack": ["usr_local"] },
    "ownerAllowFrom": ["clickclack:usr_owner"]
  },
  "channels": {
    "clickclack": {
      "enabled": true,
      "baseUrl": "http://127.0.0.1:8080",
      "token": "ccb_proof_redacted",
      "workspace": "wsp_local",
      "defaultTo": "channel:general",
      "allowFrom": ["usr_local"],
      "replyMode": "agent"
    }
  }
}
```

Commands run after the patch:

```bash
OPENCLAW_HOME=/tmp/openclaw-pr86203-proof HOME=/tmp/openclaw-pr86203-proof \
  node dist/index.js gateway --port 18799

body='{"accountId":"default","query":"/st","senderId":"usr_local","channelId":"chn_general","channelName":"general","limit":5}'

curl -sS -o /tmp/pr86203-unauth.json -w '%{http_code}\n' \
  -X POST http://127.0.0.1:18799/clickclack/commands/suggest \
  -H 'content-type: application/json' \
  --data "$body"

curl -sS -o /tmp/pr86203-auth.json -w '%{http_code}\n' \
  -X POST http://127.0.0.1:18799/clickclack/commands/suggest \
  -H 'content-type: application/json' \
  -H 'Authorization: Bearer proof-token-redacted' \
  --data "$body"

restart_body='{"accountId":"default","query":"/restart","senderId":"usr_local","channelId":"chn_general","channelName":"general","limit":5}'

curl -sS -o /tmp/pr86203-restart.json -w '%{http_code}\n' \
  -X POST http://127.0.0.1:18799/clickclack/commands/suggest \
  -H 'content-type: application/json' \
  -H 'Authorization: Bearer proof-token-redacted' \
  --data "$restart_body"
```

Observed result:

```text
unauth_status=401
auth_status=200
auth_restart_status=200
```

Parsed response summary:

```json
{
  "unauth": {
    "suggestions": [],
    "error": { "message": "Unauthorized", "type": "unauthorized" }
  },
  "authSlashSt": {
    "query": "/st",
    "suggestions": ["/status", "/stop", "/steer", "/crestodian", "/fast"]
  },
  "authRestartAsNonOwner": {
    "query": "/restart",
    "suggestions": [],
    "emptyText": "No matching OpenClaw command."
  }
}
```

This verifies the running gateway route now requires gateway auth, returns normal slash suggestions for an authorized ClickClack sender, and does not suggest the owner-only `/restart` command to a non-owner sender.

Not tested yet: a browser-level ClickClack UI autocomplete flow. This PR is still draft until feature acceptance/maintainer direction is confirmed.

## Validation
- `pnpm test:extension clickclack` (5 files, 22 tests passed)
- `pnpm tsgo:core`
- `pnpm tsgo:extensions`
- `pnpm tsgo:extensions:test`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check ...` on changed files
- `git diff --check origin/main..HEAD`
- `pnpm build`

## Validation notes
- `pnpm test:contracts:channels` initially failed on stale `node_modules/.experimental-vitest-cache` temp-file ENOENT. After moving that cache aside, it reran and failed on existing `qa-channel` contract metadata: `expected undefined to be 'qa-channel'` for `plugin.meta.id`. That failure is outside ClickClack and does not appear caused by this PR.
- A broad focused Vitest invocation against individual ClickClack/command tests hit the known local ClickClack plugin-timing spin; `pnpm test:extension clickclack` is the successful extension lane used instead.
- `codex review --base origin/main` was attempted but local Codex auth failed with Responses API `401 Unauthorized` / missing bearer auth, so no Codex review findings are available yet.

## AI-assisted disclosure
This PR was prepared with Codex assistance. I understand the code paths changed here: shared command metadata now exposes usage/menu data for autocomplete, and ClickClack has a gateway-authenticated backend suggestion resolver/HTTP route that filters suggestions through sender and owner authorization boundaries before returning command metadata.

target.title: Fix TUI Esc abort stale busy state
target.body:
## Summary

- Clear `pendingOptimisticUserMessage` after a successful TUI Esc abort, alongside `pendingChatRunId`.
- Track submitted prompts as TUI pending drafts keyed by the generated run id so early Esc abort can redact the pending transcript row and restore the text into the editor before gateway registration.
- Preserve/reconcile pending user rows across history reloads, and clear the draft-restore candidate as soon as chat/lifecycle/history proves the run is registered or durable.

Fixes #86199

## Verification

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/tui/tui-command-handlers.test.ts src/tui/tui-session-actions.test.ts src/tui/tui-event-handlers.test.ts src/tui/components/chat-log.test.ts src/tui/tui.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: TUI Esc abort could clear the pending run id while leaving `pendingOptimisticUserMessage` set, causing the next prompt to be blocked as busy even though a second Esc reported `no active run`. During the earliest submit window, a same-session history reload could also erase the just-submitted prompt before the run id was fully bound. Early Esc abort now restores the still-unregistered prompt to the editor instead of leaving it as a lost transcript row.

Real environment tested: macOS arm64, Node v22.22.0, OpenClaw worktree `tui-contradicting-esc-pattern`, commit 21f2ff2ccd.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/tui/tui-command-handlers.test.ts src/tui/tui-session-actions.test.ts src/tui/tui-event-handlers.test.ts src/tui/components/chat-log.test.ts src/tui/tui.test.ts
git diff --check
```

Evidence after fix:

```text
Test Files  5 passed (5)
     Tests  168 passed (168)
```

Observed result after fix: the regression coverage verifies that aborting a pending run clears both local busy-state fields, pending user prompts survive same-session history reloads when history has not committed them yet, pending prompts reconcile to a single history-backed row once the submitted prompt appears in history, and chat/lifecycle registration clears the local draft-restore candidate.

What was not tested: no live interactive TUI PTY run was executed; coverage is focused unit coverage for the state transitions behind the stale busy lock, early prompt disappearance, and early-abort draft restoration.

target.title: Fix local embedding worker safety
target.body:
## Summary

- Isolate local memory embeddings in a worker process by default so native `node-llama-cpp` teardown failures do not take down the gateway.
- Keep local embedding configuration unchanged; this PR does not add a `memorySearch.local.gpu` key or a QMD CPU policy knob.
- Degrade safely on local worker failure, activate configured embedding fallback with a reindex, and keep FTS-only search available when no fallback is configured.
- Keep worker-failure helpers internal to memory-core instead of expanding the public plugin SDK surface.

## Verification

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager-embedding-timeout.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/index.test.ts packages/memory-host-sdk/src/host/embeddings.test.ts` passed, 3 files / 44 tests.
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs packages/memory-host-sdk/src/host/embeddings.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager-embedding-timeout.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/search-manager.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/qmd-manager.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/index.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager.mistral-provider.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager-targeted-sync.test.ts src/config/config.schema-regressions.test.ts src/config/schema.help.quality.test.ts src/agents/memory-search.test.ts` passed on rerun except one packed-run timeout in `search-manager.test.ts`; `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/memory-core/src/memory/search-manager.test.ts` then passed, 33/33.
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.core.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core-44202-local-embedding-safety.tsbuildinfo` passed.
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.extensions.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/extensions-44202-local-embedding-safety.tsbuildinfo` passed.
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.plugin-sdk.dts.json --declaration true --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/plugin-sdk-dts-44202-local-embedding-safety.tsbuildinfo` passed.
- `node scripts/tsdown-build.mjs` passed.
- `git diff --check` passed.
- Built bundle scan confirmed `dist/memory-core-local-embedding-worker.js` exists, the worker filters `--input-type`, no `gpuLayers`, `QMD_FORCE_CPU`, or local GPU policy names are emitted, and child close handling does not call `process.disconnect` after close ack.
- `PNPM_CONFIG_PM_ON_FAIL=ignore PNPM_CONFIG_VERIFY_DEPS_BEFORE_RUN=false PNPM_CONFIG_OFFLINE=true pnpm plugin-sdk:api:check` still fails locally because this checkout regenerates `@openclaw/fs-safe` source paths differently under `node_modules`; comparing generated current vs generated `origin/main` showed only that existing path-normalization difference and no memory/embedding API surface diff.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Apple Silicon local embedding worker crash risk from native `node-llama-cpp` / Metal cleanup is isolated from the gateway process without adding a new public config value.
Real environment tested: Apple Silicon macOS 26.4 arm64, Node 24.16.0; temporary installed `node-llama-cpp` package with the macOS arm64 Metal optional package present; local `ollama` was present but not part of this crash path.
Exact steps or command run after this patch: copied built `dist/` into `/tmp/openclaw-local-embedding-smoke`, installed `node-llama-cpp` there, imported the built embeddings chunk, and ran `createLocalEmbeddingProvider()` through the default worker sidecar using `hf:ggml-org/embeddinggemma-300m-qat-q8_0-GGUF/embeddinggemma-300m-qat-Q8_0.gguf`, then called `embedQuery()` and `close()`.
Evidence after fix: the Apple Silicon smoke returned `provider=local`, `dimensions=768`, `magnitude=1`, `elapsedMs=457`; the parent process survived worker startup/use/close. Focused tests also simulate worker exit code 134 and prove structured degradation/fallback/FTS behavior.
Observed result after fix: local embeddings default to worker isolation; configured fallback activates and reindexes after a search-time local worker crash; no fallback keeps keyword search available; `memorySearch.local.gpu` is rejected by config validation; public plugin SDK exports do not include local-worker-only failure helpers.
What was not tested: the exact reported native Metal teardown crash was not reproduced locally; LM Studio and vLLM were not on PATH and are not part of the `node-llama-cpp` Metal cleanup path fixed here.

target.title: fix(agents): count exec-path openclaw cron add in successfulCronAdds
target.body:
### What

This PR fixes an issue where the `successfulCronAdds` counter was not incremented when an agent ran `openclaw cron add` as a shell command via `exec` or `bash`.

### Why

The existing logic only checked for the structured `cron` tool. This caused the agent runner to misread the counter and incorrectly append "I did not schedule a reminder" to the transcript after a successful shell-based cron addition.

### How

An `else if` block is added to `pi-embedded-subscribe.handlers.tools.ts` to inspect `exec`/`bash` results. It uses an anchored regex (`/^\s*openclaw\s+cron\s+add\b/i`) to identify the command and requires a success receipt (`created|scheduled`) in the output before incrementing the counter.

This approach incorporates the hardened regex requested in the prior (self-closed) PR #53024. New regression tests are included to cover the fix and prevent bypasses.

Closes #52972

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed:** Agent runner incorrectly appends "I did not schedule a reminder" after the agent runs `openclaw cron add` via exec/bash shell tool (structured `cron` tool path was unaffected).

**Real environment tested:** OpenClaw development clone, Node 24, Linux (WSL2). Exec/bash tool path exercised via the handler directly.

**Exact steps or command run after fix:**
1. Agent issued `openclaw cron add "daily standup" "09:00"` via exec tool.
2. Exec result contained `Reminder created` in the aggregated output.
3. Inspected `ctx.state.successfulCronAdds` after handler returned.
4. Checked whether the "I did not schedule a reminder" note was appended to the transcript.

**Evidence after fix:**

Before fix — `successfulCronAdds` stays at 0 after a successful exec-path `openclaw cron add`, triggering the false "did not schedule" note.

After fix — handler reads the exec result, matches `/^\s*openclaw\s+cron\s+add\b/i` on the command, finds `created` in the aggregated output, and increments the counter. Bypass attempt (`echo "openclaw cron add ..."`) correctly stays at 0 because the anchored regex rejects substring matches.

Console output (branch-local run):
```
✓ increments successfulCronAdds when exec runs openclaw cron add and output confirms creation
✓ does not increment successfulCronAdds for a bypass attempt (substring match without anchor)
✓ does not increment successfulCronAdds when openclaw cron add output lacks creation receipt
✓ does not increment successfulCronAdds for a cron add command without the openclaw prefix
  24 passed
```

**Observed result after fix:** Counter increments on valid `openclaw cron add` via exec; false "I did not schedule a reminder" note is suppressed. Counter stays 0 on bypass attempts, failed runs, and unrelated commands.

**What was not tested:** Live OpenClaw server with real cron backend and actual scheduled jobs; agents using non-standard shell wrappers around `openclaw cron add`; exec timeout paths.
